pdf_name: Günbatti, Cahit - Die Beziehungen zwischen assyrischen Kaufleuten und anatolischen Fürsten aus Kültepe. CDOG 8, 2008 125-136.pdf
page_text:
130
Cahit Günbatti
Fürsten und Assyrern von großer Bedeutung ist, da hier auch zum ersten Mal
von einem Fluß-Ordal die Rede ist.
Der assyrische Kaufmann Aššur-takläku war von dem lokalen Palast fest-
genommen worden. Zwei Monate später trat eine Delegation des kdrum vor
den Fürsten und die Fürstin und sagte, daß der verhaftete Aššur-takläku
unschuldig sei und sie um seine Freilassung bäten. Die Vertreter baten um
eine Gelegenheit, damit der verhaftete Assyrer seine Unschuld beweisen
könne, indem er entweder vor dem Dolch des Gottes Aššur schwöre oder
wie ein Anatolier zum Fluss gehe. Sie sagten sogar, daß sie selbst anstatt
Aššur-takläku vor dem Dolch Aššurs schwören könnten. Für die Freilassung
des Verhafteten stellten der Fürst und die Fürstin eine Bedingung, nämlich
die Auslieferung eines anderen Assyrers, der Kontakte mit dem feindlichen
Fürst von Tawiniya unterhalte und gegen das Fürstenpaar tätig sei. Falls die
Vertreter das nicht erfüllten, sollten sie ihnen (dem Fürst und der Fürstin) l
Mine amûtum oder 10 Minen Gold bezahlen. Im Falle, daß diese Forderun-
gen nicht erfüllt würden und der Name der im Dienst des Fürsten von
Tawiniya stehenden Person nicht verraten werde, drohten sie die Hinrich-
tung Ašsur-takläkus an.
Offensichtlich griffen der Fürst und die Fürstin, die alle Vorschläge der
Vertreter des keirum ablehnten, zum Mittel der Erpressung und verlangten
eine enorme Summe, ein halbes Kilogramm amûtum oder fünf Kilogramm
Gold, für die Freilassung des Gefangenen.
Dieser Text ist auch ein anschauliches Beispiel für die Verpflichtung der
Verwaltungszentren der assyrischen Kolonien in Anatolien zum Schutz der
Rechte ihrer Staatsbürger gegenüber den lokalen Verwaltungen.
Wie wir aus anderen Texten erfahren, konnten die assyrischen Kaufleute
wegen Unruhen oder Konflikten zwischen den anatolischen Fürstentümern
nicht immer ruhig ihren geschäftlichen Aktivitäten nachgehen, und sie waren
oft nicht ihres Lebens sicher. In Kt 92/k 526 9 lesen wir die folgenden Worte,
die die bedrohliche Lage der Kaufleute widerspiegelt: 6-10)... annakam
rubci'um i sikkatim magitma ina qerab A/im ki sihitim saknat u ana gagqadd-
tini pa/hani „Hier kehrte der Fürst von den sikkatum zurück. In der Stadt gibt
es einen Aufstand, deswegen fürchten wir um unser Leben".
Diese und andere ähnliche Beispiele sollen nicht sagen, daß alle lokalen
Fürsten gegen die Kaufleuten oder ihre Autoritäten gnadenlos und brutal
gehandelt haben. Obwohl die Zahl der Beispiele dafür gering ist, lesen wir in
einigen Texten, daß manche Fürsten mit den Kaufleuten geschäftliche Ver-
Günbatti 2001, 158 n. 2; Çeçen 2002. 66-67.

er (ungestört) gehen und kommen!
Ich, (17) bin ich denn ein Verbündeter, wenn ich an ihn (sc.: den Herrn) herantrete, (18) mein
Herr aber ihm (sc.: mir) nicht schreibt?! (19) Und wenn es der Tatsache entspricht, (daß) mein
Angebot nicht (20) gut (genug) war, (dann) möge mir mein Herr die Vorstellung meines Herrn 11
(21) schreiben, damit sie (meine) Speise vor mir (22) essen! Dort (Rückseite) (23) hat aber
hoffentlich nicht irgendein (anderer) deren Hände ergriffen und (sie) jemandem [anderen] (24)
übergeben! Was könnte mir mein Herr dafür zurückgeben?
(25) Wenn es mein Herr für gut hält, (26) dann möge mir mein Herr (stattdessen) zwei qallu-
Sklaven —sei es einer von der Kullâ, (27) sei es einer von der Esaggilu, oder einer von irgend-
einer anderen (Dame) aus (28) deren Mitte, die mein Herr für gut hält — (29) es kann (aber auch)
ein améluttu-Sklave meines Herrn (30) oder der Tirutu oder der Qibit-tübi (31) oder jemandes
seines Beliebens sein— herführen und kommen? (32) Ich, ich werde die Verantwortung für sie
tragen12 !
[Und w]enn (33) es mein Herr nicht für gut hält [und] mein Herr (34) nicht kommen wird: Es
gibt viele Babylonier, (35) die mich hier kennen — einem von (36) ihnen möge mein Herr (ihn)
geben, damit er (ihn) herführe und (37) mir übergebe! Ich, ich werde hier alles Geld für sie, (38)
das man für sie aufgebraucht hat, erstatten!
(39) Aus Not habe ich fürwahr schon an die siebenmal wegen eines améluttu-Sklaven (40)
meinem Herrn geschrieben! Mit Ah1i6-gullim (41) und umâ möge mein Herr einen améluttu-
Sklaven hersenden! (42) Dann werde [ich] nach bestem Bemühen die Verantwortung für sie
tr[agen!]
(43) Mein Herr sollte den améluttu-Sklaven nicht vom Auszug [zurückhalten!] (44)
(Stattdessen) möge mein Herr (jemanden) senden, sei es einen a[mêluttu]-Sklaven (oberer Rand)
(45) (aus dem Haus) meines Herrn oder einen qallu-Sklaven: (46) Er (Sie) soli (sollen) ohne
Verzögerung kom[men und] (47) die liginnu-Tafeln studieren!
1.3. Philologische Bemerkungen
Z. 4, 8: Die Redewendung ki asmû umma, die in diesem Nippur-Briefkorpus auch noch in
2:4, 20:16-17, 29:5, 96:10 und 99:4 belegt ist, bietet hinsîchtlich ihrer Konstruktion und ihrer
Deutung Schwierigkeiten: Sie ist zusammengesetzt aus der Subjunktion ki "wenn, etc." und dem
Prt. 1.Sg. von semû im Subjunktiv; da sie nicht ein Nebensatz mit vorangehendem oder nachfol-
gendem Hauptsatz ist, sondern mit umma "folgendermaßen:" auf einen Hauptsatz überleitet, ist sie

THE CENTRAL BLACK SEA REGION
111
As mentioned above, for the localisation of Zalpa in the recent years two names
came to the fore: first ikiztepe, then Oymaagaç Höyük. Based on evidence from our
recent surveys, we are of the opinion that a third name should be taken into consideration
for the possible localisation of Zalpa. Presented above and in the catalogue with its rich
finds from the Middle Bronze Age, the geographic location of PaSaSeyh Tepesi also has
features that can be identified with those of Zalpa. Pameyh Tepesi is only 10 km of
bird's flight away from the Kizilirmak/Marassantiya (Fig. 28) and it is also situated on
the terraces of a lesser tributary (Fig. 10) to this river. Besides, it is about 20 km away
from ikiztepe which is known to have been located on the Black Sea coast close to the
mouth of the Kizilirmak/Marassantiya in the first quarter of the second millennium BC.
As inferred from these geographic data, Pa$a5eyh Tepesi, which is located at half a day's
walk to the KxziltrmaklMaraccantiya and at a day's walk to ikiztepe, is a possible
candidate for ancient Zalpa.

was convinced that “elders” formed part of the puhrum.20 He argues that the “elders” served
as witnesses, since they knew the local inhabitants and their business well.21

P. Koschaker and A. Ungnad argue that since Old Babylonian texts sometimes operate with a
separation of city (ālum) and elders (šibūtum), Walther’s identification of puhrum, elders and
city is untenable: The city and the puhrum were sometimes interchangeable terms, and the
elders had some relation to the puhrum, but the exact working of the puhrum is difficult to
assess because the sources are few.22 The institutions of elders and the city will be discussed
further below.

In the legal documents VS VII 149 and CT VIII 19a, the puhrum is the place where cases are
presented, perhaps at a public place where all the citizens are gathered. This would explain the
role of the assembly as the place for public punishment. The public nature of the puhrum as a
place of adjudication will be discussed further below. The document VS VII 149 further
reports that the city reached a verdict. This seems to indicate that the puhrum was the
assembly, whereas the “city” was some more limited council that reached the verdict, perhaps
identical with the witnesses, among whom was the rabiānum or “mayor”. The nature of the
puhrum will be further discussed under the literary sources to Old Babylonian institutions.

13.2.2 The rabiānum or “mayor”
In Old Babylonian documents, a person called a rabiānum frequently occurs together with
institutions such as the “city” or the “elders”. He also appears on lists of witnesses. The term
rabiānum means generally “the chief” and is often translated “mayor” or “burgomaster” for
sedentary settlements.23

In the Codex Hammurapi, the rabiānum is mentioned in two clauses. Codex Hammurapi §23
states that in the case of robbery where no perpetrator has been found, the robbed awīlum, or
“citizen” shall declare himself before the god (mahar ilim), and then the ālum u rabiānum,
“city and mayor”, shall make good the lost property.24 Further, Codex Hammurapi §24 states

THE ARCHIVE OF KULIYA

ilar is said of the groups of tablets of 68 and 69, which makes us guess at their purpose. More such
data18 from excavated archives may shed some light on how the scribes and owners of archives
handled and registered their tablets.

Whatever the purpose of these lists, the fact that none of these legal documents was found in the
archive makes it rather likely that they were taken out to be saved, because no trader would throw
these valid debt-notes and title-deeds away. And it is attractive to connect this with the abandonment
of the house shortly before the destruction of the k£arum. Anyhow, their absence means that we lack a
lot of valuable information on the archive and business of Kuliya.

Another somewhat disturbing piece of information must be mentioned here. In her letter 11:20ff.,
sent from Assur, Kuliya’s wife tells him: “Everything I gave him for A. has been registered on a wax
tablet” (ina †uppim ßa ißkurim lapput). This first and thus far only reference to such a tablet (see the
note on 11:22) implies that, at least in Assur, such tablets were used for administrative purposes and
we cannot exclude the possibility that simple references to †uppum, “tablet”, could also refer to such a
tablet, since there usually would have been no reason to specify which type of tablet was meant. We
do not know whether they were also used in Anatolia, but the fact that such tablets did not survive
anyhow means that part of the written evidence is lost.

2. Dating the archive

The period during which Kuliya was active is clear from the eponymic datings in his archive. A
general picture, based on the texts and the eponymy list KEL A I had made available to him, was
already drawn in Kryszat 2004, ch. 2.1, especially p. 17 with table 3. Using the new data on the length
of the gap following the end of KEL A, we can now state that texts in his archive contain the
following eponymic datings (the numbers are one higher than those in Veenhof 2003a)19: 106, 124,
126, 129 (2x), 130 (4x),20 131, 132, 134, 135 and 136 (2x). The first and earliest date occurs in the
verdict 70:6f., which confirms a claim by Kuliya’s mother, apparently an older document which he
had obtained or inherited, perhaps because the debt had never been paid. In four of the records (39:7f.,
44:11f., rev. 15’f., 45 rev.: 17’f., 44 rev. 15’f.) and presumably also in 47:11f. (its lines 14’f. feature a
be®£ul£atu-loan given out by him) Kuliya figures as creditor and in 46:10f. and 29f. he appears as
debtor. Kuliya is also mentioned or involved in the transactions described in the letters 14 and 22 and
in the record 35. This means that his activity is documented during the eponymy years 124 to 136. He
might also be the anonymous tamk£arum of the fragmentary 49:9’ (year 130), but this is uncertain in
37:7 and 45:rev.7’ (years 126 and 130), which contain the clause “the bearer of this tablet is the
creditor”, which leaves the possibility open that Kuliya had obtained these tablets when the original
creditor had ceded or sold them. The second text is the reverse of a large memorandum, which in lines
12’ff. records a debt claim with Kuliya as creditor. The latest dates (eponymy year 136) are in 39:7-11

Aßßur is given; tablet 20, son of Ali-ahum IKraya; tablet 21, Ali-ahum ßa Hahhim. But note also some

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
TELL ESH SHEIKH CHRONOLOGY
Woolley in his references to the excavation at Tell esh Sheikh describes the
black burnished wares of its lowest levels XII-XI as Halaf in type, and notes the
appearance of imported painted sherds mixed with local painted ware already in
level XI.35 The local style continues from levels X-I, a period of time estimated as at
least 300 years. Cultural continuity with the nearby site of Tabara al Akrad is
established in its lowest level VII, where Tell esh Sheikh local ware occurs beneath
the Uruk period levels VI and V. The dating of the entire sequence of Tell esh
Sheikh levels X-I to late Chalcolithic Ubaid is suggested by D. French's recent
study of the pottery in the Antakya Museum.36 It is during this time that the seals
are produced, which in the Amuq sequence would be contemporary with late phase
E (or transitional to F?).37 The gable seal first appears in Amuq E, with a geometric
pattern; the latter phase F provides closer parallels for designs on the Tell esh
Sheikh seals.38
The site of Tepe Gawra in northern Mesopotamia, particularly levels XII-XI/
XA as we have seen, produces a number of parallels for both the forms and motifs
of Tell esh Sheikh seals. These include, from the dump, a pyramidal seal with
concentric rectangles (see no. 1); a level XIA rectangular seal with angle-filled cross
and human figure and sealings with humans (see nos. 3, 5); level XIA circular seals
with angle-filled crosses (see no. 4); level XII sealings with horned animal related to
nos. 7 and 13; level XIA-XI sealings with bucrania (see no. 12); and a level XA seal
with a flat-style animal related to a group which includes no. 9.39 This represents a
span of time which is interrupted by a violent destruction at the end of XII, a phase
which contains both the typically Ubaid 4 painted wares of XIII and the Uruk
monochrome unpainted pottery that characterizes levels XIA-VIII.40 However, as
Buchanan already noted in 1952, this division is not reflected in the glyptic material
and the seals of levels XII-XI (preceded by transitional phase XIII) belong to a
single artistic tradition which carries on through level VIIIb.41
Culture period III at the site of Degirmentepe-Malatya has been dated by
Esin to the "late" Chalcolithic period (Ubaid II/Early Uruk) on the basis of
pottery associations with Tell esh Sheikh, Tabara al Akrad VII, Amuq E-F, Coba
Hoyiik IVA-C, Tepe Gawra XIII-IX and Nor?untepe period IV.42 The seals and
sealings came from the rooms and courtyards of a large building complex.43 Their
designs relate to those of Gawra XII-XI, although exceptional sealings are
impressed with Halaf pendants and Gawra XIII well-style figural motifs.44 The
angle-filled cross motif and lentoid form of Tell esh Sheikh seal no. 4 and the leaf
pattern of no. 10 are paralleled at Degirmentepe. However, most significant is the
appearance at Tell esh Sheikh of a carinated hemispheroid seal (no. 11), which
matches not only the preferred shape and material of seals at Degirmentepe, but is

Kronolojik olarak, bu sorunlarla başka olayların bağlantıları kurulabilir. 113-115 numaralı

sorun, Sadaya'nın oğlu Assur-idI'nin dahil olduğu sorun gibi, Salim-Assur'un ticari bağlantılar
kurduğu tüccarların ölümünden kaynaklanmış görünmektedir. Üstelik İbaya'nın oğlu İddin-Suen,
Purushattum'da yüksek bir memur olan Usinalam ile Salim-Assur'un çekişmesi hakkındaki me-
tinlerde tekrar ortaya çıkmaktadır.
64 Dolayısıyla, bir dizi olayın da yaklaşık aynı zamanda, yani
REL 88-92 (1885-1881) arasında meydana gelmiş olması muhtemeldir.
Eğer bu yeniden kurgulama doğru ise, bu yıllar Salim-Assur için çok yoğun geçmiş ve bu
problemler ciddi ekonomik baskılar içermiş olmalıdır. Birkaç yıllık süre içinde meydana gelen fi-
nansal ve yasal problemlerin böyle bir birleşimi sık karşılaşılan bir durum olamaz. Diğer taraftan,
bu problemlerin Salim-Assur için mali kayıplara sebep olup olmadığı ya da ne kadar sebep olduğu
açık değildir. Bu büyük dosyaların Kanis 'teki evin arşiv odasında tutulmuş olması dikkate değer­
dir, çünkü Salim-Assur bir 15 yıl kadar daha yaşamıştır ve belgelerin aile ile ilgili hiçbir alakası
kalmamış olmalıdır.
Salim-Assur'un ne zaman kalıcı olarak Durhumit'e taşındığı bilinmemektedir, fakat Usina-
lam ile çekişmesinde kötü bir şekilde sonuçlanan iş zaten onun bakır ticaretine olan büyük ilgisi-
nin bir işaretidir. Taşınmasının, REL 88-92 arasında meydana gelen olaylarla bağlantılı olarak ya
da bu olaylardan kısa bir süre sonra gerçekleşmiş olabileceğini sanıyorum. Onun artık Kanis 'te
yaşamadığı gerçeğinin, belli ki, bazı sonuçları olmuştur; bunlardan biri belki de arşivin durumu-
dur. Çünkü görünüşe göre, alakasız, eski metinler, betimledikleri olaylardan çok sonra bile arşivde
tutulmaya devam etmiştir. Onun en büyük oğlu Ennam-Assur'un, o zamandan itibaren, Kanis'teki
evde kendi ailesiyle birlikte yaşadığını varsaymalıyız. Oysaki küçük oğul (Ali-ahum) bu evde
yaşamamış gibidir.
Salim-Assur 94 arşivi dışındaki belgelerde birkaç kez görülmektedir. Geçenlerde yayımlanan
Assur-re'I arşivindeki (AKT 7a) bazı belgelerde şahit olarak geçer; bu belgelerden birinde yıl ta-
rihlemesi (REL 82), fakat diğerlerinde sadece hamustu-tarihleri vardır.

known Sumerian literary texts. One possible, if not likely, explanation for presumably Semitic
scribes writing in Sumerian is that they belonged to a mixed ethnic, bilingual community. It is a
suggestion that finds support in the observable grammatical interference. As is well known, the
Akkadian conjunction u and preposition in alongside some month names and number words al-
ready make their appearance in the Ab„ ŒalΩbÏæ administrative texts.59
The presence of these words, in particular the conjunction u, has solicited considerable
comment and there is a common assumption that already at this early date Sumerian was toiling
“under a heavy Semitic influence.” 60 But it is important to put this evidence within its proper ty-
pological context. As is well documented, when languages with conjunctions come into contact
with languages without — and here it must be noted that Sumerian -bi-da, literally “with that/its”
is of limited productivity, being used only with nominals — conjunctions are among the first ele-
ments to be borrowed.61 Thomason and Kaufman have produced a borrowing scale that is meant
to serve as a rough, probabilistic indicator of the degree of contact; on a scale of 1 to 5, with 1
indicating “Casual contact: lexical borrowing only” and 5, “Very strong cultural pressure: heavy
structural borrowing,” the borrowing of function words is a 2, and prepositions a 3.62 Thus, while
the presence of the conjunction u and the preposition in certainly speak to early interference and
bilingualism, they can hardly be marshaled as evidence for particularly strong interference, let
alone for the terminal stage of Sumerian.
As may already be discerned by the assumptions that the conjunction u has engendered, the
prevalent view is that during the third millennium Sumerian was in a type of free fall, as if the de-
cline of the language could already be detected, that the trajectory to its death was already mapped
out. Kienast has gone so far as to claim that Ur III grammar strongly suggests that Sumerian was
already a dead language during this period,63 while Thomsen states that “the language of the non-
canonical texts like documents and letter-orders, which presumably would be close to the spoken
language, are very much influenced by Akkadian, thus indicating that the Neo-Sumerian scribes
did not have Sumerian as their mother tongue.” 64 The only evidence given for this claim is the
distribution of the prefixes mu- and ba- in Ur III texts, which is a different issue altogether, one
having nothing to do with mother tongues or the status of Sumerian as a living or dead language.
This is not the place to delve into the intricacies of Sumerian grammar; suffice it to say that in our
texts many of the alleged corruptions are actually issues of writing or may find more likely ex-
planations in the ordinary evolution of the language — that languages are in a state of continual,
internally-motivated change is a fact that is too often overlooked in discussions of Sumerian
grammar. Certainly there are errors, and moreover borrowings, but Ur III Sumerian does not in
any way bear witness to the massive structural and lexical interference that signal the death knell
of a language. It is critical in this regard to distinguish malignant language decay, which is diag-
nostic of language death, from normal language-contact phenomena such as borrowing, which
involve healthy, stable languages.65
Indeed, from the perspective of our textual evidence, Sumerian died with “its morphological
boots on,” to use Nancy Dorian’s felicitous description of East Sutherland Gaelic, a language that
is dying without loss of structure or intensive borrowing.66 In fact — as Michalowski points out,

There are at least three phonological innovations that occurred in Lycian but
not in Milyan.80 These are *s>h (except next to certain consonants), *w>Ø before
syllabic resonants, and *k’>t. Compare, for example, the possessive suffix Lyc. -ehe/i-
vs Mil. -ese/i-, the suffix of ethnic adjectives Lyc. -ñne/i- vs. Mil. -wñne/i-, and Lyc. ti
vs Mil. ki ‘who’.81 I am not aware of opposite types of correspondences, which would
illustrate phonological innovations of Milyan with respect to its close relative Lycian. In
all the three cases cited above, Carian shares the archaisms of Milyan, cf.. oton-os-n
‘Athenian (acc. sg.)’, kbd-yn-ś ‘Kaunians (acc.pl.)’, and the etymological relative
pronoun kﬂi functioning as a linker in nominal phrases (Adiego 2007: 371, 377, 392). The
morphological innovation that unites Milyan with Carian, as opposed to Lycian, is the

Easton, Hawkins, Sherratt and Sherratt
entire fortified lower city, complete with buildings.
While this may be defended as simply offering a
suggestion of what may well have been the appearance of
the Late Bronze Age city, it may just as well be criticised
for greatly exceeding the available evidence.
A step further was taken in the exhibition, which
presented a large model or maquette showing the entire
restored citadel and lower town. This seems to have had
the effect of bringing into the open a strand of academic
dissatisfaction with Korfmann's work. This was articu-
lated principally by a Tiibingen university colleague of
Korfmann, the ancient historian Frank Kolb, who in a
book published in 1984 had characterised Troy as a
'miserable little settlement', which could not 'raise a
claim to the designation as a city'. Interviewed on the
subject of the Troy exhibition by the Berliner
Morgenpost, he defended his position against the impli-
cations of Korfmann's reconstructions, describing them
in such terms as 'fiction', 'figments of fantasy' and 'the
media hot air balloon of the Troy excavations', and
accusing Korfmann of deliberately misleading the
public. His remarks found a ready audience in the same
media at the start of a long hot summer, and Kolb was
encouraged to sharpen his offensive and language,
branding Korfmann the 'von Daniken of Archaeology',
and employing other such unacademic barbs. All this
took place while Korfmann was out of the country, on
excavation at Troy.
Kolb claimed to be speaking for a significant number
of German academics, who kept their views to
themselves for fear of accusations of envy, clearly not a
charge to which he felt himself vulnerable. His offensive
was joined by Dieter Hertel, Privatdozent at the Institute
for Classical Archaeology in Munich, who had worked
with Korfmann at Troy and now published a booklet,
Troia. Archdologie, Geschichte, Mythos (2001). This
work plays down the significance of the site of Troy and
of Korfmann's operations there.
Kolb's aggressive and intemperate language more or
less speaks for itself. It did not find favour with the
Rector of Tiibingen University who demanded a public
retraction and apology.
The university further
convened a 'scientific symposium' under the title 'The
meaning of Troy in the Late Bronze Age', which took
place on 15-16 February 2002 before a large and
excited public audience, and was attended by consid-
erable media coverage. There were 13 invited speakers,
approximately paired to put the cases for the 'prose-
cution' and 'defence' in the spheres of archaeology and
excavation, trade and the environment, the historical-
geographical background of the Hittite texts and the
Homeric problem. Theoretically at least, ample time
had been allowed for audience participation and
comment after each paper, but as always this depends
on the speakers keeping to time, which is of course the
exception rather than the rule. The hopes of the Rector
and convenors of the symposium for a calm academic
debate of the important questions where personalities
and invective would be set aside were only very
partially fulfilled.
The site of Troy has the misfortune to stand on not
one but two academic fault-lines, one on either side of
the Aegean: the Homeric problem concerned with the
historicity (or otherwise) of the Iliad; and the problem of
Anatolian historical geography of the Arzawa lands as
reconstructable (or not) from the Hittite texts. Both
topics have been known to evoke strong emotions from
those involved, and anyone working at Troy will have
difficulty in keeping clear.
The authors of this article attended the symposium as
invited observers, and had some opportunity to make
their English voices heard amid the often heated German
exchanges. Since each of us is a specialist in one aspect
of the subject, we thought that it would be of interest to
readers of this journal to see our assessment of the
respective debates and our own views on the issues. We
thus offer our presentations under the headings (with
authors' initials):
The archaeology of the site: citadel (JDH) and lower
town (DFE)
The historical geography of western Anatolia in the
Hittite texts (JDH)
Bronze Age trade in western Anatolia (AGS/ESS)
While each of us has obviously drafted one section,
we have each read the others' contributions and offered
comments which have been incorporated as appropriate.
The archaeology of the site
The citadel
The 'prosecution' has devoted effort to denying that the
site of Troy could represent a 'Residenzstadt'. Their
argument is generally conducted by setting up criteria for
such an entity and then demonstrating that Troy does not
meet these.
The grounds on which Troy is denied this status are:
(1) the size and character of the walls, gates and
surviving buildings; (2) the lack of finds of materials
expected of a palatial centre, such as written documents,
seals and sealings, luxury goods, traces of wall paintings,
sculpture etc. This line of argument is advanced princi-
pally by Kolb 2002b, and by Hertel in his paper at the
Tiibingen symposium (Hertel 2002). They are of course
much aided in this argument by the total disappearance
of the greater and most significant part of the citadel,
which has been noted above.

« Dis-moi ce que tu bois… »
201
2.1. L’eau
L’eau, mû, représente la boisson par défaut des anciens Mésospotamiens, « la
nourriture et la boisson » étant souvent exprimées en akkadien par akalum u mû, « pain et
eau »20. Boisson naturelle, l’eau ne fait pas l’objet d’un traitement particulier ni n’est
l’objet d’un négoce ; en conséquence, elle n’est quasiment jamais mentionnée dans les
textes. L’eau était puisée dans les fleuves et les canaux, parfois à l’aide de machines
élévatoires, ou amenée dans les localités par un système de canalisations21. L’eau destinée à
la consommation était tirée d’un puits et conservée dans des citernes ou de grands vases où
elle décantait22 ; elle était servie de préférence fraîche (kasû), dans des jarres (karpatum) ou
des outres (nādum).
Dans la correspondance privée assyrienne, l’eau, comme sujet du verbe « boire »,
apparaît principalement dans des expressions imagées ; la plus fréquente, « boire de l’eau
sur le dos de quelqu’un », signifie « profiter d’une situation au détriment d’un tiers » et a
peut-être pour origine l’usage partagé des canaux d’irrigation23. Rien n’est dit sur la
manière dont les particuliers se procurent l’eau qu’ils boivent.
Les archives royales de Mari sont en revanche plus prolixes sur le sujet et permettent
par exemple de comprendre comment la capitale était approvisionnée en eau. Des lettres
font état de la recherche de sources d’eau pour l’agriculture et l’alimentation ; dans la
campagne, les champs étaient arrosés à partir de puits-daluwātum24. L’administrateur d’un
district de la vallée du Habur, alors qu’il s’assure que l’eau d’une source est utilisable,
découvre une seconde source et s’inquiète aussitôt de savoir si elle peut être exploitée25. La
ville de Mari disposait non seulement de bassins réservoirs de part et d’autre de la
Grand’Porte, mais aussi d’une conduite de dérivation (amrummum) qui permettait
d’amener l’eau du canal à divers réservoirs situés à la Porte du roi ; ces réservoirs (abrum)
servaient à l’alimentation en eau du palais26. C’est sans doute là que venaient les puiseuses
d’eau chargées de rapporter, peut-être à dos d’ânes, les grandes quantités d’eau nécessaires
à la cuisine, aux bains et aux plantes du palais27. D’autres puiseuses d’eau, rattachées aux
cuisines, ne sortaient pas du palais : elles s’approvisionnaient dans le réservoir (iggum) de
la cour 106 ou dans la citerne de la cour 131. Le réservoir de la cour 106 était rempli par
l’eau de pluie ainsi que l’indique une lettre envoyée au roi par Šibtu28 : « Le 24, une averse
est tombée à Mari. Dans l’intérieur du réservoir que mon Seigneur a fait, l’eau a empli une
canne » ; celle-ci décantait avant d’être consommée.
L’eau sous forme solide était également recherchée, employée pour conserver les
aliments et rafraîchir les boissons. Le ramassage de la neige et la construction de glacières

mu-ti-ma (RIMA 1, 14: 10) 'for ever' according to A.K Grayson, apparently equating ana
mu-ti-ma with (ana/adi) matema (§ 12.6.3); ma-afl--ri-Ti-ma 'earlier, before'(?) (RA 81,
65: 35);25 ina ma-TA and i-ma-TA (AKT 6C, 542: 4, 543:12; 634: 20; AKT 6D, 733: 16
and EL 325a: 9(?), very uncertain, possibly not a temporal adverb); and sar-ra-nu-
ma (CCT 5, 13a: 22), which Michel (2001a: 278 n. d) translates 'premierement' (cf.
sarruum 'to begin') but which may rather be a PN. For e$is 'soon, quickly' and kayyanis
'continuously', see § 13.5.5.
13.5.4 Adverbs of manner (except-is-derivations from adjectives)
-kenatem(ma), the oblique feminine plural of kenum 'true, reliable, honest,' is
common as an adverb 'truly, honestly, in good faith', e.g.:
ke-na-te-ma naruqqam apturma kunukkuka
'in good faith I have opened the sack, but ( it turned out to have) your
seals' (ICK 1, 153: 12-14).
The same meaning is expressed by ina kenatem (VS 26, 117: 24-25), perhaps
also ina kenem (AKT 6B, 395: 12), and by the Ace Masc Sg kenamma (ke-na-ma
AKT 6B, 310: 24; m/k 14: 11).26
- kiam ( spelled ki-a-am or ki-am) and kiamma ( spelled ki-a-ma or ki-a-am-ma
(TPAK 1, 6: 6)) 'thus, like this, in this/that/the same manner', and Just so, simply,
anyway' (Veenhof 1976: 156-157). For the latter meaning, see, e.g.:
(here I am free (to work for) your and my interest but) ki-a-ma wasbaku
'I am sitting idle' (BIN 4, 38: 18, lit. Just so')
25
The meaning seems clear enough, but this is the only instance of a derivation of the root
mbr with temporal meaning in OA ( which normally uses panium and its derivations). The
form may come from * mabratum ( as opposed to warkatum) with the adverbial suffix -e <
*-ay, or with a ls suffix-i, parallel to warkat + suffix pronoun ( mabrit- is less likely because
mabrium is not attested in OA).
26
An adverb seemingly derived from kenum is Kl-na-kam in: ina GN tezibannima ina qa-t{
mu-tern Kl-na-kam U-${ (h/k 73: 3-5), which Michel (2006: 170) translates: 'et vraimentje
suis sortie de l' esprit (litt. des mains) de mon mari'. However, apart from the ambiguity
of mu-tern (mutum 'husband' or mu.tum 'death'), Kl-na-kam 'vraiment' is problematic,
since the suffix -iikam is always locative in OA. Since Ass -kam regularly corresponds to
Bab -kiam (Kouwenberg 2012: 54 ), Kl-na-kam may be the OA counterpart of the adverb
* kinnikiam 'there', that occurs in Mari Old Babylonian as kinnikem ( o.c. 65 n. 121 ), and is
then to be interpreted as * kinniikam. Thus perhaps: 'you left me behind in GN, and there
I have escaped from the hand of death'. Further instances are required to confirm this.

44, based on a parallel passage in Cylinder A from Ashur6, proves to be eorreet. Note the reduplieation of the third
radical in li-hal-liq-qu (line 44), the inseription's very last word.7 In line 38 the text offers se-pu-u-a instead of ana
GIRI1-ia found in the drafts-tablet KA V No.171, 13. The new text provides us with the missing verb at the end of
line 37, which ean be restored: 8 kur-bu LUGAL-u-t[i hu-ud lib-bi tu-ub UZU.M]ES na-mar ka-bat-ti a-na da-ris ki-i-
[na], 'bless my kingship, grant [me happiness, good heal]th (and) bright mood forever' .9 The appearance of kunnu
in line 37 makes the earlier restoration of the end of line 36 (k[i-in ana $at1])10 rather questionable. The k[1] there
is more likely to be the beginning of a noun, semantieally elose to nannabu, for whieh I suggest kisittu or kimtu.
Line 36 would then read: 11 ur-rik u4-me-i[a MU.AN.NA.]MES-ia su-[um-d]il na-an-na-bi k[i-im-ti] I k[i-si-it-ti ru-up-
piS], 'prolong m[y] days, ex[ten]d my [year]s, [spread wide] my progeny (and) fa[mily] I des[eendants]'.12
Texts No. 6 and 7 are fragments of Esarhaddon's Cylinder B from Kalhu, presented in a partial edition by
A.R. Millard, Iraq 23 (1961) 176-178. Cylinder B differs from Cylinder A (Borger, Esarhaddon §21) in its building
account, being of a more general nature, in its apocopated benedietion formula, and in its date (676 as opposed to
672 of Cylinder A). The preserved signs on Texts No. 6 and 7 agree with all of these eriteria. Now, Text No. 6
follows direetly on Text No. 7, and judging from their identieal physieal deseription,13 an indirect join seems
eertain. To the bottom left of Text No. 6, K 1659 - one of the unpublished fragments listed by R. Borger as
duplicates of Cylinder A14 - ean be further joined. Sumer 44, 154, No. 7(+)No. 6+K 1659 reads:15

Conclusion
With the exception of one probable gap between c. 100 B.C. and the 4th cen-
tury A.D., and one possible gap between c. 600 and 300 B.C., occupation at Kilise
Tepe seems to have been continuous from the Early Bronze Age until the 6th or
7th century A.D. The scant evidence for the Middle Bronze Age in the surface
survey does not seem to be reflected in the excavations, where this period seems
to be well represented. Early Bronze Age levels have also been reached in the
excavations but the depth of deposits beneath these levels would indicate the
possibility of Chalcolithic occupation although no pottery of this period was
recognised in the surface survey.
7. EXCAVATED MATERIAL: PRE-CLASSICAL POTTERY (D. Symington)
The pottery repertoire encountered during our first season at Kilise Tepe
can be described as a pleasing assemblage in which a large percentage of pottery
types received some form of surface treatment and painted wares were not
uncommon. As already observed by Mellaart (1955, 1958) and D. French
(1965), through their respective survey work in the Goksu valley, the ceramic
affinities of Kilise Tepe, then known as Maltepe, lay mainly with Cilicia to the
south. Future investigations should define this trend more clearly, but our first
impressions would largely confirm their findings. Pottery parallels can frequently
be drawn with the sequence of Tarsus and Mersin. The fact that Cilicia itself
was subject to alternating influences over the millennia coming from central
Anatolia and North Syria respectively, would also have affected the Goksu
valley, since Cilicia's contact with the plateau was not only maintained through
the Cilician Gates, but equally via the valley of the G6ksu. Cultural relations
with the Konya plain to the north, on the other hand, cannot be fully evaluated
at present since we still lack a complete ceramic sequence for that region which
would allow a comparative analysis for the Kilise Tepe material.
Much of the pottery corpus retrieved in 1994 awaits more detailed study
and the following represents an outline of the main pottery groups as we
encountered them in the course of the excavation.
Iron Age (Figs. 14-15)
Besides the surface finds, the Iron Age pottery recovered originated mainly
from a series of pits in K20 and J20, as well as mixed deposits in the latter

50

Large tablet with some damage. Its first part (until line 19) seems to be a deposition made before the
trading station of Íaladuwar by three persons (who also mention there absent colleague), who had
earlier on witnessed a confrontation between two parties, one of which consists of more than one
person (line 11, -kun£utini). But since the text continues after line 19 without marking the beginning of
a new record and uses a second person sing. (l. 23, ku£atima), it might be a long letter (note the first
person sing. verbs in lines 29, 32, 36, etc.) in which the just mentioned deposition is quoted. Its
continuation mentions a number of transactions (perhaps quoting from the relevant contracts),
including several in which the speaker/writer entrusted (apqid) amounts of silver for transport, with
mention of the witnesses in whose presence this happened (lines 26, 33f., 38f., 47f.), once also of a
date (eponymy year 131, line 40f.). Note in lines 35f. (cf. 49), “I set these witnesses against them in
Nehriya when I entrusted to them the packets” (with silver, mentioned in lines 27-32, cf. l. 48). It is
not clear who is speaking, not Kuliya who figures in line 15 in the third person, and there seems to be
a discrepancy between the second pers. sing. masc. in l.e. line 1 (ß£Èbka) and fem. in line 53
(uß£ebilakkim). For that reason I have ranged the tablet among the memorandums. Since the lines are
long I present transliteration and translation not in two columns.

06) Return to CBS 1766 -
In JANES 30 (2006) 37-53, the article by W. Horowitz, "CBS 1766: A Late
Babylonian Tablet with Concentric Circles from The University Museum," presented a preliminary edition and
discussion of a tablet with a diagram of a seven pointed star inscribed inside two concentric circles, followed
by a numerical tablel). Soon after, in N.A.B.U. 2007, pp. 43-45, no. 40, C. Waerzeggers and R. Siebes proposed
that CBS 1766 be identified as musical text, more specifically as "a visual tuning chart." This identification
was made in great part by a breathtaking interpretation of the labels attached to the points of the star as the
names of the seven first strings of the harp. This new interpretation has been greeted most favorably. J. Friberg
accepts this identification in a soon to be published article.2) Likewise, A. Kilmer included a discussion of
:. CBS 1766 in a January 2008 lecture on rnusical texts at a conference held at The Biblelands Museum,
·. Jerusalem, where she emphasized that CBS 1766 would be the earliest exarnple discovered thus far of a musical
. treatise represented by a circular diagram with star points.3) Yet, as Waerzeggers and Siebes freely and fairly
admit in their N.A.B. U. piece, their new readings include emendations that are not based on actual collations of
the tablet. On a recent visit to The University Museum in Philadelphia, we had the opportunity to recheck and
recollate the original tablet as part of a !arger project involving the place of CBS 1766 in Ancient
Mesopotamian civilization.4) Given the great interest in the tablet, we give at this early date a summary of our
collations from our visit to Philadelphia making use as well of a number of photographs taken on a digital
camera from different angles and distances.5)
Collations of The Labels of The Points of the Star
1 .

THE YEAR'S WORK
11
The attempt to find those milestones which are known in the literature
continues with some success: many, however, seem to be lost. Almost all the
museums in the area have been visited; notes, photographs, squeezes have been
taken of a substantial number. Many new stones can now be added to the corpus.
The list of caput viae and Governors' names has been amplified and, in one case,
a previous mis-reading of a name has been corrected.
I would like to thank the Directorate General of Antiquities for the several
permissions which have enabled me to carry out the work.
In collaboration with the Museum of Anatolian Civilizations in Ankara,
I have been able to ensure the safety of a number of stones, by arranging either
for their transport to Ankara or, in some cases, for their preservation in the care
of village authorities.
DAVID FRENCH.
I spent the Long Vacation 1974 on leave from the School of Oriental and
African Studies travelling in Turkey, with a three-week break for a visit to Syria
and the Lebanon. I continued my work of collating and photographing, and
taking squeezes, where necessary and feasible, of Hittite Hieroglyphic texts in
the regional Museums. Thus I worked in Istanbul, Kayseri, Adana, Antakya and
Gaziantep Museums, and visited the Museums of Ankara and Mara? to make
arrangements for a subsequent spell of work. I also visited the site of the now
lost K*tiikale inscription with a view to a future attempt to recover it.
I had the opportunity for valuable discussion with Turkish colleagues
including Professors Alp, Alkim, Balkan, gambel, Kalaq, Nimet and Tahsin
Ozgiiu and Bay Raci Temizer. Professor Tahsin Ozgi*q was kind enough to give
me access to the recently discovered Kululu economic texts, on which I am
writing a report for him.
DAVID HAWKINS.
For much of my year as Fellow, I was incapacitated by hepatitis. I have,
however, been able to carry work on the large number of A?van grain samples to
an advanced stage, and publication of the results should be possible in the near
future. When illness took me to Britain for hospital treatment, I was able to
examine more of the Tell Abu-Hureyra plant remains and prepare a fairly
detailed report for publication in Proc. Prehist. Soc.
In early summer, I undertook field work in the Aksaray-Nigde-Nev?ehir
and Yozgat regions, accompanied by Miss Christine Dent of the Department of
Agricultural Botany, Reading. The purpose of this work was principally to collect
extra samples of seeds of legumes which are needed (as reference material) in
identifying some of the more problematic legumes in the plant remains from Can
Hasan III. The large number of seed samples which we gathered are now in the
BIAA plant reference collection.
In July, I was invited to join the late Professor Young's colleagues at
Gordion in order to examine the plant remains which they had accumulated over
the years. I was able to complete the preliminary identification of all the
available samples.
GORDON HILLMAN.
From October 1973 to October 1974 I was based at the Institute in Ankara,
supported by a Leverhulme Overseas Studentship. My intention was to achieve
field work for my thesis on Byzantine architecture and architectural sculpture in

IX. QUALIFICATIONS OF TEXTILES
surprise if a substantial part of the textiles exported by the Assyrians
were of "cheap quality". Many kutıznû, a carefully made product, were
qualified as spa qdtim. That BIN 4, 65, 16f., quoted above sub b., p. 195,
states that some of the textiles, classified as "good" were matiû, and there-
fore only counted as spa qdtim does not prove they were "of inferior
quality" ; matium only means they failed to reach the standards set for
"good", which did not make them automatically "cheap". An additional
argument for a meaning "of normal, current quality" is that sa qdtim has
a semantic parallel in Sumerian g e n, "current" used at about the same
point in the scale of qualities, cf. below p. 209f.
Besides §a qdtim we meet a number of times a word written gdtum ; cf.
e.g. BIN 4, 185, iff.: 162 TÛG . HI . A qd-TUM 20 TÛG . HI . A SIG5 DIRIG. One
might be tempted to read qadum, and translate : "162 textiles, including
20 extra fine pieces", as a phrase "x textiles-a qadum (qadï in TC 3/2,
134, 9' ; BIN 4, 61, 4; ATHE 62, 5) y textiles-b" is frequently attested.
But it is primarily used to indicate that among a lot of textiles a smaller
number can be singled out because of their function ("for wrapping") , or
their owner ("of the freighter") and not to state the composition of a load
of mixed textiles. In the latter case the use of sA . BA, "thereof .. ", is pre-
ferred cf. ATHE 37, 34; TTC 14, 6 and the texts quoted in CAD A, 1, 35b
sub a) . Moreover when we read in GARELLI-KENNEDY 1960 no. 4, 3: 324
TÛG qıd-TUM 5 TÛG a-4ıa-ma, an interpretation qadum, "including", seems
excluded by ahamma, "separately", which already indicates the presence
of a separate lot. The same text in line 7 distinguishes this big lot of tex-
tiles from 13 pieces of "good quality". This suggests that qd-tum serves as
an equivalent of spa qdtim, also denoting "of current quality".
This same gddum can be found in BIN 4, 7, 8: 300 TÛG qd-tum ro TÛG
dam-qıi-tum; ICK 1, 53, 2: 151 TÛG qd-tum 9 ku-ta-nu sIG5; CCT 3, 49a, 5:

I me-at ku-ta-nu qd-tum ıo a-bar'-ni'-ıi, followed by 14 TOG ku-ta-nu 51G5
ıo TÛG spa a-ki-di-e. Note that in BIN 4, 185, 7-9 we twice have ı TÛG qd-
tum spa PN.
The interpretation of qadum = spa qdtim is supported by two facts:
a) in nearly all cases gdtum is found in an enumeration of various
qualities in ascending or descending order, and it always occurs next to
damqum/SIGs as one would expect for spa qdtim.
b) the word following qadum is normally in the nominative, and not in
the genitive, which qadum, "including" would require 321).
One may explain this gatum in two ways. It may be taken as a loc. adv.,
821) CAD A, z, 35, a reads in CCT 3, 49a, 5 end : qd-dum x a-bar (copy : me)-nil-ıi,
but this is grammatically incorrect, as one would expect a-bay ni-e.

"decree" concerned in particular the somewhat more complicated case of sustenance fields
assigned by the crown to officials who, as absentee land-owners, in turn had to contract ten-
ants to exploit them. 80 We might even consider the possibility of a more general decree deal-
ing with crown-land and the duties and rights of its owners and tenants (both iššakkü and
errë ü), in which case there might even be a link with the case treated under c. Lack of back-
ground information makes it impossible to say what exactly the substance of the royal decree
was. Perhaps it dealt (also) with the sharing of economic goods in such relations, since viola-
tion (dal-as-um) of such rights, e.g. on irrigation water, occured according to AbB 4, 23:17f.,
13, 119:10f. and 199:9 (where in the first and third letter tenants are involved).
e. Decree on groundless claims
Groundless (ina la idim) claiming of property (bagarum/bagrii) is regularly forbidden in
contracts, especially in those of conveyance of real property and slaves, and in verdicts, which
may also impose or stipulate a penalty for it. There is even a verdict from Dilbat (VAS 7, 152),
dated to Hammurabi's twelfth year, where the dismissed plaintiff, if he tries again, will
receive "the penalty for who raises a (groundless) claim" (at-an baqiranim). This suggests the
existence of a generally known penalty for such claims, perhaps including those raised after
an earlier renunciation of claims. 81 Such a penalty may have been rooted in customary law,
but the letter AbB 1, 14 (quoted in § 2) shows that there existed a written (royal) decree on
how to punish such a wrong claim.
Of the Old Babylonian law collections only LH deals a few times with claims on prop-
erty, in all cases in order to prohibit them in particular situations. Claims raised by pre-
vious owners or third parties on property bought only occur in connection with slaves, in
§279. Other claims treated are those by a husband on his dead wife's dowry, in §§162-3,
those on children born from a marriage between a slave and a free person, in §§171 and
175, those by brothers on real property donated by their father to their unmarried sister,
in §179, and those on adopted children, presumably by their natural parents, in §§185-
188. 82
AbB 1, 14 deals with a lawsuit concerning the inheritance of an (unmarried) woman of the
naditu-class, "which Naram-ilišu, the brother of Ibbi-Samaš, had acquired", and which was
contested by "the men of (the town of) Durum". The latter lost the case, because the court
upheld Ibbi-S'amaš's title to the inheritance, which must have meant he had the right to sell or
deed it to his brother. The men of Durum were punished for their action "in accordance
with the text of the tablet with the decree, as if they had claimed what was not theirs".
LH §§ 178-9 treat the question what happens, after the death of a father, with the real estate
he had donated to a daughter who as a priestess, naditum or sekretum stayed unmarried. With-
out written authorization to deed the property to whom she prefers, the real estate returns to
her brothers in exchange for a lifelong maintenance; if they fail to provide it, she retains its
8° see for references to the miksum of such fields CAD M/2, 64.b. An added compiication could be that the
fields had been acquired by Hammurabi after his conquest of Larsa and were assigned to the king's servants
together with the tenants which used to work them, who now have to acknowledge him as their new owner.
81 see R.A. Veenker, An Old Babyionian Legal Procedure for Appeal, HULA 45 (1974) 1-15 and Dombradi
1996, I, 345ff., §§459-465.
82 see for a detailed analysis of this issue Dombradi 1996, I, 262-294, and on its occurrences in iaw coliections,
278f., §376, where she discusses the difference between ragdmum and bagarum.

Nach unserer Auffassung verfügt also diese Stelle über zwei miteinander in Verbindung
stehende Bedeutungsschichten. Erstens: Sie scheint auf Lugale Z. 34-38 anzuspielen, wo das
Herausragen des ú-Steins als König über die Armee von Steinen angesprochen wird; die Textstelle lautet
wie folgt „Es (= Asag) erschuf Nachkommen in den Bergen, machte seinen Samen breit. Sie (= die
Nachkommen) nennen (ihn) einstimmig, den Schmirgelstein ihren König; (und) er erhebt (seine)
„Hörner“ unter ihnen wie ein großer Stier. Der šu-u-Stein, der saĝ-kal-Stein, der esi-Stein, der ú-si-
um-Stein, der ka-gi-na-Stein (und) der Held ĝešnu, diese Helden plündern die Städte.“19) Zweitens: In
der Metapher des allesglättenden, bzw. -bohrenden und „für alle Steine notwendigen“ Schmirgelsteins, in
der auch der Grund für seine Wahl als König der Steine in Lugale Z. 34-38 liegt, verbindet sich die
Aussage über die auch technisch gemeinte Weisheit von Lipiteštar (d.h. der König, der u.a. auch der
Steinschneidekunst kundig ist – Lipiteštar A Z. 82-86) mit seiner Bedeutung für das Rechtsleben des
Landes, indem er, wie der Schmirgelstein von der Oberfläche der Steine, alle Unregelmäßigkeiten
beseitigt (Lipiteštar A 88-97).

*) Dieser Beitrag ist im Rahmen meiner durch ein Stipendium der Aktion Österreich-Ungarn finanzierten
Forschungen an dem Institut für Orientalistik der Universität Wien zustande gekommen. Ich bedanke mich bei
Professor G. J. Selz für seine zum Nachdenken anregenden Anmerkungen über die wahrscheinliche metaphorische
Bedeutung von Lipiteštar A Z. 87 und dafür, dass er das Manuskript meines Beitrags gelesen und verbessert hat.

1) Vgl. SAZONOV 2007: 335. Hier müssen aber zwei Tatsachen in Betracht gezogen werden. Erstens:
Lipiteštar war nicht der erste König, der die Position „Sohn von Enlil“ für sich beanspruchte; seit der Herrschaft von
Narāmsîn sind Könige bekannt, die sich auf diese Weise bezeichneten (ANNUS 2002: 13-20; SAZONOV 2007: 333-
336) Zweitens: Neben Ninurta war auch Nanna der erstgeborene Sohn Enlils, was darauf schließen lässt, dass sich
der Titel „Sohn von Enlil“ besonders in dem Fall der Herrscher der dritten Dynastie von Ur ebenso gut auf Nanna
beziehen konnte (ANNUS 2002: 17). Zu der Behandlung der Uruk-zeitlichen und frühdynastischen

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
whose base lies some seven kilometres to the south. The country is rough and
broken, with numerous small and ferile basins for cultivation and abundant fodder
on the slopes for grazing. Characipolis lies about seven kilometres north, over
another range of hills.
Daldis contains no standing buildings but many traces of them in the
numerous fragments and spoils which appear on the site and in the neighbouring
villages. Although Buressch reported only inscriptions, Keil and Premerstein saw a
decorated sarcophagus, architectural fragments and numerous double-edged
columns as well as other pieces indicative of a Christian date.3' The place was
plainly of some importance through Late Antiquity.
The acropolis of the city consisted of a hill cut off by stream beds on three
sides, but neither very steep nor high.32 It stands in the midst of gently rolling
country with scattered oak and poplar trees, much scrub, and frequent cultivated
fields of rather stony soil. Lacking a strong natural defence, the hill was once
fortified with walls whose poor and overgrown remains partially survive. Their
Byzantine origin is manifest in their facing of large roughly coursed fieldstones with
a filling of brick, some of it in parallel courses, and all set in a good deal of grey
mortar. This undistinguished style is common in a wide region from Lydia and
western Phrygia through Mysia and Bithynia, and seems to be of very late date,
most probably the thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries, the last period of
Byzantine rule.33 Dating is especially difficult here, because the walls are poorly
preserved, and sections which may have had more regular and hence more
diagnostic masonry have been extensively robbed.
Coins seen in the village in 1971 were of all periods from the Roman through
the Byzantine, with numerous local issues of Sardis of republican and imperial
date, an abundance of Roman coins of the third through fifth centuries, a few
pieces of Justinian and Heraclius, and a good lot of autonomous Byzantine issues
of the tenth through the thirteenth century. All were of bronze, except for one
denarius of Augustus. By their sequence, the coins attest the continuing life of the
city from Antiquity through the Middle Ages, but do not reflect the proposed date
for the walls. Instead, they raise a different possiblity. Walls such as these have the
appearance of hasty constructions, perhaps of an age of crisis. The Turkish
invasions which obliterated Byzantine rule in the area were such a time, but so also
was the period of the first Turkish attacks of the late eleventh century, followed
immediately by civil war, collapse and gradual Byzantine reconquest. It is possible
that walls of this kind could date from that time. Considerations of style and
parallels with dated fortresses, however, make the later date more probable,
whatever the explanation for the apparent lack of late coins. The bishops' lists, in
any case, indicate that Daldis still existed in the late thirteenth century.
ATTALEA AND ITS CASTLES
The name of the Lydian city of Attalea indicates its Hellenistic origin; the
Attalid Eumenes I founded it after his victory over the Seleucid Antiochus I near
Sardis in 261 B.C. A settlement for soldiers who could profit from the agricultural
wealth of the region, Attalea never achieved more than local importance, being

33See for the moment Foss and Winfield (1987) 163 f; such walls will be discussed in a
future report of the survey of Medieval Castles of Anatolia (British Institute of Archae-
ology, Ankara).

Verpflichtungsscheine mit erkennbarer Causa. 	 77
Béra-(i)di mit Assur-rabi als Gläubiger. Beachtlich ist die kàrum-Zinsklausel Z.
10ff., vgl. obenVorbemerkung vor Nr. 73. Nach Z. 15 ff. ist, falls der Schuldner eine
(Geschäfts)reise vor der Fälligkeit der Schuld ordnungsmäßig beendet hat, das
von ihm geschuldete Silber von seiner „Verköstigung", d. h. wohl von seinem
Lohnanspruch abzuziehen. Danach scheint Béra-(i)di sich zu einer Geschäfts-
reise für Assur-rabi verpflichtet zu haben ; Asssur-rabi hat ihm vor Antritt
der Reise 14 Segel Silber bezahlt, worüber Béra-(i)di den vorliegenden Ver-
pflichtungsschein ausstellt. Bei dem Silberbetrag kann es sich um das ganze
Entgelt oder nur um einen Teilbetrag handeln. Indem der Schuldner die
Reise ordnungsmäBig durchführt, erbringt er die von ihm geschuldete Gegen-
leistung. Dabei braucht der Schuldner nicht volle 10 Fünfteljahre zu reisen;
es kommt vielmehr nnr auf die richtige Vollendung der Reise innerhalb
dieser Zeit an. Die in Z. 8 ff. angedrohten Verzugsfolgen werden erst dann
angewandt, wenn der Reisende die Reise nicht fristgerecht erledigt hat. Es
handelt sich also, wirtschaftlich gesehen, um einen sogenannten „fiktiven"
Verpflichtungsschein. Vgl. auch dazu Nr. 97 f. und BIN IV 211 (unten
Nr. 163).

oi.uchicago.edu/OI/DEPT/PUB/SRC/OIP/131/OIP131.html
brown loam had been washed from the low limestone plateau to the south. Small freshwater gastropods within the
lower horizons suggest that these soils were irrigated by perennial flow from spring-fed pools, presumably those at
Imma, which in turn had also supplied water to turn several water mills similar to those indicated on figures 2.27–30.
Although the mill sites are indicated on the French series maps of the area, these mills have now been obscured by
modern buildings. Dating of the mollusk-containing soil horizons was by means of numerous Late Roman/Early Byz-
antine sherds (second–sixth centuries A.D.) concentrated within sediments characterized by pedogenic blocky struc-
ture within the mid-lower profile (fig. 2.9). No evidence indicates that soils were deliberately introduced as backfill
from elsewhere. This aggradation, at least 3 m in 2,000 years and representing a rate of accumulation of ca. 15 cm per
century, clearly attests to the high rates of sediment delivery that must have prevailed during the last 2,000 years of the
human record in this region.
Despite the evidence for the trapping of sediment on slopes, within tributary valleys and behind agricultural walls,
a significant amount was transported through the system to accumulate on the floodplain of the Orontes River, where it
probably contributed to the deep sequence of levee sediments (Wilkinson 2000).
DIMINISHING ARCHAEOLOGICAL RECORD: PHYSICAL TRANSFORMATIONS
One major problem that hinders the recognition of archaeological sites and landscape features is that such features
are often lost or obscured by physical or cultural processes. Unless we are aware of these processes and how they oper-
ated through time, it is often difficult to estimate how much of the archaeological record has been lost. In terms of
physical action, two primary processes appear to have reduced the archaeological record of the Amuq region:
(a) The erosion of sites by rivers in the plain, or by overland flow and minor channels in the uplands.
(b) The obscuring of sites on valley floors as well as on the plain itself as a result of the deposition of allu-
vium and colluvium eroded from the surrounding uplands.
It is of course impossible to quantify the losses of the archaeological record, but by conducting geomorphological
studies alongside the archaeological survey it has been possible to identify key loci of erosion and sedimentation as
well as general areas of site loss. Figure 2.4 shows the general pattern of sedimentation on the plain, which as was
pointed out in the 2000 report (Yener et al. 2000b), was remarkably patchy with large areas of deep sedimentation
along the rivers and alluvial fans, as well as areas of low or zero sedimentation in parts of the plain. Recognition of this
sedimentary patchwork goes some way toward countering Woolley’s gloomy assessment that sedimentation made ar-
chaeological survey a worthless exercise (Woolley 1953a).

Z. 17) Der Titel rabi kalbätim ist zuerst belegt in AKT III, 41: (17) IGI Si-pu-na-ah-§u-u§
(18) GAL kà-al-ba-tim, „Vor Sipnunahgug, dem Verantwortlichen für die Hündinnen. "
Es handelt sich hier um den Verkauf eines Dorfes oder einer Siedlung, wie wir ihm in den
Inschriften aus Kültepe selten begegnen.15 Tahisra, der Name des verkauften Ortes, treffen
wir in den Texten aus Kültepe hier zum ersten Mal an.
Auffällig ist weiter, dass drei der in dem Text genannten Zeugen den Titel rabi „Oberster,
Aufseher, Verantwortlicher für..." und ähnlich tragen. Dass bei der Ausfertigung solcher
Dokumente auch hochrangige Personen als Vertragspartner oder als Zeugen anwesend sind,
lässt vermuten, dass der Inhalt des ausgefertigten Dokuments auch die Regierung von Kanig
interessierte und das Dokument eine administrative Dimension hatte. Die Höhe der verein-
barten Geldstrafe, den die Seiten bei Verstoß gegen den Vertrag zahlen mussten, wurde auf
das Doppelte des Verkaufpreises, nämlich 10 Mine Silber, festgelegt und zusätzlich wurde die
Todesstrafe vorgesehen.

THE YEAR'S WORK
ACADEMIC WORK
It proved a fruitful year for the Institute's academic pursuits. After an initial
delay in the granting of the Tille permit, it was possible to carry out a 4-week
season in June and a 2'-months season in September-November. Permission was
also granted for the Director's continuing work on the roads and milestones of
Roman Asia Minor, this year the work being conducted in the (orum region and
Western Anatolia.
Permits were issued to Alan Hall and Stephen Mitchell for their respective
surveys at Oenoanda and Yalvaq, fuller reports on which appear below.
Antonio Sagona was successful in securing the necessary clearance to carry out
work on the Agvan Early Bronze Age material in Elazig Museum as a preparatory
measure for publication of that material. He arrived in Ankara in mid-November
with the intention of spending 21 months in Elazig.
Other academic work over the year included the preparation of the first Tille
volume (the Medieval levels) by John Moore, work by the drawing office staff for
the Can Hasan and Tell esh Sheikh publications to be produced by the Director,
and the near completion by the Director of the second volume of his Roads and
Milestones of Asia Minor series.
The culmination of previous academic work was seen in the publication
during 1983 of two further volumes in the Institute's monograph series published
by B.A.R.-Armies
and Frontiers in Roman and Byzantine Anatolia edited by
Stephen Mitchell and TaSkun Kale by Anthony McNicoll. This brings to 6 the
number of BIAA monographs published since 1980.
PREMISES
It has, fortunately, been a year without moves. The office and library remain
in the warm and comfortable surroundings of Tahran Caddesi 24 where the
Director also continues to live.
In January, a 5-year lease was purchased on Flat 21 at Biikliim Sokak 96-the
flat which accommodates the bone, botanical and squeeze collections. This at least
provides some security of tenure on that property for the foreseeable future
although it continues to be our aim to house all of the Institute's work functions
under the one roof.
LIBRARY
The arrival of our new librarian, Belkis Aktan, has enabled us during the year
to carry out some much-needed and long overdue book shelving checks and
reorganizations to increase the library's efficiency.
In November, Dr. Malcolm Wagstaff visited Ankara to discuss with the
Director and Assistant Director the future buying policy and financial administra-
tion of our library. It is to be hoped that the useful exchange of ideas will bear fruit
in expanding the library to suit both the type of Institute we wish to see and visitors
to our library.
We continue to increase our periodical holdings by exchanging periodicals for
Anatolian Studies and currently receive 84 periodicals on exchange.
ACTIVITIES IN THE U.K.
The lecture programme in London opened at the AGM in March, when
Professor Clive Foss, who is Director of the Institute's survey of Medieval Castles

ATHE 11, the document listing the value in silver of the accounts of nine Absentees
that has been discussed in 7.1.5.2, mentions three different quantities:
8 minas 25 shekels, the standard one as described in the caption and credited to six
persons;
4 minas 12 112 shekels, half of the standard quantity and credited to Kilia and Sfi-
Kfibum ;
11 minas 20 shekels, more than one third higher than the standard quantity and only
credited to KSu-ken.
The credited amounts together represent a value of 70 minas 15 shekels of silver. Two
thirds of the Absentees have contributed the amount of textiles and silver which had been
fixed for one share. The different amounts result in two cases from depositing only half.
This could mean that the persons concerned, Kilia and Sfi-~fibum son of Kubidi, had
been registered as a miial awilim and therefore had to pay only half of the datum. PfiSu-
ken, on the other hand, possessed a double share according to VS 26, 55, a text which
stems from a period when this trader was not in Kanish. TC 3, 154 even mentions a
fivefold share, but unfortunately the name of the shareholder is not specified there. The
evidence quoted earlier knows only of a full share, 75 per cent of it, or 50 per cent of it,
but does not speak about multiples of shares.

The understanding of TC 1, 4 is hampered by the apparent absence of other texts
dealing with the same affair. When datum-payers order Kurara to stand for the settlement
of accounts this probably refers to the official settlement in Kanish. The circumstance
that Eddin-ASSur seized this opportunity to lay a claim on half of the 260 woollen fleeces
Kurara is entitled to, shows that these fleeces result from an enterprise initiated by the
colony. A partly different interpretation was given by Veenhof, who regarded the
causative stem of iz2zum to mean that a datum-payer made Kurara act as his representa-

Der Schaden ist immens. Denn mit dem Verlust der
Notizzettel lassen sich auch keine Angaben mehr über
die Fundsituation der betroffenen Stücke machen. Die
Tafeln aus der Bibliothek des Kizir-Aschur ließen sich
nun von Schriftstücken, die an anderen Stellen in Assur ge-
funden wurden, nicht mehr unterscheiden. Erschwert
wird diese Situation noch dadurch ganz erheblich, dass
für den nach der Fundteilung nach Istanbul gelangten
Teil der Tontafeln aus Assur kein systematischer Katalog

Extraneous Influences and Origins of the Sacred Architecture in the Iron Age Judah 25

entrance is in the shorter, eastern wall. They are also known as a temple in antis,
Syrian style, Anatolian style or megaron.14
The earliest megaron temples were found much earlier in the second millennium
in northern Syria. Their inspiration came from Anatolia during the third millen-
nium (it was an open portico with columns supporting a roof), while it disap-
peared during the second millennium.15 From the Early Bronze Age in Canaan
there is evidence of small single broad-room temples (this will be discussed later,
for example at Arad). Long-room temples are from the Middle and Late Bronze
Age, for example at Tell Munbaqa and Emar (two temples were found at each
site). Tell Munbaqa is on the east bank of the Euphrates and its temples have an
additional room between the portico and the main hall, and part of it were two
columns. Emar on the west bank of the Euphrates hid two identical temples con-
stituted of a long room, an unenclosed entrance hall and columns.
Two temples at Tell Tayinat were found in the Amuq valley in southeastern Tur-
key in 1936 and 2009. Both temples are long-room types. The first one from
1936 had three chambers in an east-west orientation (antechamber, main room,
and Holy of Holies, with two columns and a pair of lions at the base and an ab-
sence of side chambers and its size is 11.75 x 25.36 m.16 The second temple that
was discovered in 2009 is in a north-south orientation consisted of three rooms:
an antechamber, with one column in front, a main hall and the Holy of Holies
without any chambers on the sides with a size 9 x 21 m.17 Both temples were
used at the same time as king’s temples and they were turned to Assyrian tem-
ples. These temples could not have been the source, or inspiration, for the Solo-
monic and Moza temples because they were dated to the 9th century BCE (825–

14 Also a description of Solomon’s Temple is a long-room type made up of ulam, hekal,
debir, a side chamber and a pair of columns (Jachin and Boaz). 1 Kings: 6,2–10: “The
house that King Solomon built for the LORD was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide,
and thirty cubits high. The portico at the front of the main hall of the temple was twenty
cubits long, extending across the width of the temple and projecting out ten cubits in front
of the temple. It also had narrow windows framed high in the temple. The chambers
against the walls of the temple and the inner sanctuary, Solomon built a chambered struc-
ture around the temple, in which he constructed the side rooms. The bottom floor was five
cubits wide, the middle floor six cubits and the third floor seven cubits. He also placed
offset ledges around the outside of the temple, so that nothing would be inserted into its
walls. The temple was constructed using finished stones cut at the quarry, so that no
hammer or chisel or any other iron tool was heard in the temple while it was being built.
The entrance to the bottom floor was on the south side of the temple. A stairway led up to
the middle level, and from there to the third floor. So Solomon built the temple and fin-
ished it, roofing it with beams and planks of cedar. He built chambers all along the tem-
ple, each five cubits high and attached to the temple with beams of cedar.”

maximum height of the tomb was 1 75 m. Its floor consisted of hardened earth and
fine gravel. In the northeast corner a small votive table, made of stones
(0-40 x 0-55 m.) once supported offerings. A 2-10m. long stone wall, running
parallel to the east wall and set 0-70 m. in front of it, and a ledge in the east wall
originally supported a collapsed wooden cover, parts of which were recovered
(Fig. 8). The remains of a young man, aged between 19 and 2013 were found in the
depression below the original bed, together with a bronze fibula, which can be
dated to the fourth century A.D., several pearls, fragments of bottles and of a large
one-handled jug, also dated to the fourth century A.D.14 This fourth century date
was also confirmed by a contemporary lamp (P1. XL d), placed just outside the
door.'5 However, the other finds indicate that this was not the original date of the
vaulted tomb. The squashed remains of at least six more people were discovered
against the west and north wall. They seem to belong to another male adult (aged
between 22 and 30), to two females (one between 23 and 51, the other between
35 and 44) and to an adult whose sex has not yet been established. The tomb also
contained the remains of two children of approximately six and ten years old. The
tomb had been robbed in antiquity but many of the original contents still
remained, including two simple golden earrings belonging to different pairs, a
bronze ring and pendant, many pearls and seven complete or fragmentary small
perfume bottles (P1. XLI a), which could be dated to the second/third century A.D.16
There was also a silver denarius of Commodus, dated to the autumn of A.D. 177.17
It thus became clear that the tomb was first built during the later second or earlier
third century A.D. as a family tomb and that it was still used or reused in the fourth
century, a situation which is similar to that of the tomb excavated last year. It
supplies further proof that this whole area contained tombs, as well as a mixture of
workshops, kilns and potter's dumps.
The second trench (trench II), opened 6 m. to the west of the first one in a
southward direction, showed that the whole slope was divided by a succession of
small, parallel terrace walls, four of which were discovered during the campaign.
The highest terrace contained a small dump (F 13). On the second terrace a very
homogeneous dump (F 14), the remains of which were eroded down the slope,
contained over 3 tons of rejected pottery, probably all belonging to the early
Imperial period and the first half of the first century A.D. (P1. XLI b).
Below this dump on the third terrace from the north, nine burial urns had been
placed against a terrace wall. Cooking pots, jugs and jars had been used for this
purpose, seven of which were almost complete, some of them with drinking vessels
used as lids (P1. XLI c). The cremated remains of six burials have been studied by
Dr. Christine Charlier. They all belong to young adults (aged between 16-18,
17-19, 18-25, 18-30, 20-40, 25-40), two of whom were women. It is clear that we
are dealing here with a collective tomb, laid out after a catastrophe, and not with a
family tomb. The row of burial urns probably continues in both directions. One is a

A REINTERPRETATION OF THE MILAWATA LETTER
19
Perhaps Atriya was left unharmed so as not to leave Iyalanda without frontier
defence in the border zone it may well have shared with Milawata.
Returning to the Milawata letter, we should reconsider several important
pieces of information:
(1) If Hoffner's reading of line 47 is correct, the Hittite king and M had gained
possession, through military assault, of at least the border territory of Milawata.
Whether or not this was followed by a more extensive conquest of Milawatan
territory, there can be little doubt that Milawata was at this time regarded by the
Hittites as enemy territory.
(2) M's father was an avowed enemy of the Hittite king. This is made clear both by
a general statement in the letter (Yourfather, who continually desires my misfortune,
and who is [to My Majesty] the chieffactor among the evil factors),27 as well as by
the father's refusal to hand over the hostages of Utima and Atriya. From the
Tawagalawa letter we note that Atriya was a fortress lying in the Lukka territory of
Iyalanda, and in all probability Iyalanda lay close or adjacent to the border of
Milawata. Quite possibly Utima was also a fortress in the frontier area. Fur-
thermore we note that M's father "boasted" the city of Arinna. Although the sense
of this is not clear, it may indicate a conquest or takeover of Arinna. Of the several
towns or cities called Arinna in Late Bronze Age Anatolia, we know of one that lay
in or near Lukka territory.28
And within the context of the Milawata letter, this
may have been the city of which M's father "boasted".
(3) From the last section of the letter, we note that at the time of writing the
hostages from Utima and Atriya were in the hands of M, from which we can infer
that at this time M occupied the position previously held by his father. This would
suggest that either he had succeeded his father, or else had taken control forcibly
from him. The latter seems to me much more likely, as we shall consider below.
On the basis of the above information, I think it not at all unlikely that we are
dealing in the letter with events in and around Milawata, and that Milawata was in
fact the land ruled by M, and by his father before him. If we are prepared to allow
this as a reasonable proposition, we might then reconsider the letter as a whole,
with particular reference to the role played by the rulers of Milawata in western
Anatolian affairs during the latter half of the 13th century.
To begin with, it is clear that M's father was involved in activities beyond the
confines of his own territory, as reflected in his association with Arinna, and in his
acquisition of hostages-Hittite subjects presumably-from Utima and Atriya.
The letter gives no indication of how or in what circumstances the hostages had
come into his hands. Possibly they were acquired as the result of some military
action in the border region between Milawata and Lukka, especially if Utima like
Atriya was a fortress settlement. If so, the hostages may have been NAM.RAMES
or
"booty-people", carried off in the aftermath of a military engagement.29
At all events the refusal of M's father to restore the hostages may well have
been the main reason, or one of the main reasons, for the Hittite military response
indicated by line 47 of the join piece (if we accept Hoffner's new reading). In
27Lower
edge, lines 1-2.
28Referred
to in KUB XXIII 11 and 12 line 7. See Bryce, JNES 33 (1974), 399-401.
29We might ask whether there is any real distinction between a NAM.RA (a "booty-
person") and a L'Ll (a "hostage"). A conqueror uses the term NAM.RAMES
in reference to
people he has conquered
in battle and carried off to his own land. On the other hand, a king
whose subjects have been taken from him in this way might not regard them as permanent
acquisitions
of the enemy, but simply as "hostages",
retrievable
from the enemy either by
exchange or negotiation, or by a further military operation.

KLAAS R. VEENHOF
tamkarum have been repeatedly discussed, but there exists neither a full agree-
ment nor a comprehensive analysis of the many uses and meanings of this term
in Old Assyrian trade. The issue is not only why and how the term is used in
particular contexts, but more generally whether the term, basically "trader, mer-
chant," is primarily functional or refers (also) to a person's profession or sta-
t ~ s . ~ ' )
Garelli, while admitting that a tamkarum plays different roles in specific
situations, qualified him as "not an ordinary trader," but "une sorte d'inter-
mediaire agrCC, chargC de faciliter les transaction^."^^) Others doubt such a
status,43) and prefer a functional interpretation, proposing translationslinter-
pretations conditioned solely by the role a tamkarum plays in a particular
context: as head of the Anatolian branch of a firm, as the person actually
travelling and trading, hence "tractator" or sales agent, as creditor, as money-
lender (especially in the combination bdt tamkarim, "house of a money-lender,"
where one takes out loans), or as somebody who may raise a claim (in sale
documents)."")
We can trace several situations in which a creditor or owner of silver or mer-
chandise remained anonymous by being designated as tamkarum.
- Investments in a "naruqqu-society" (see above) could be "registered to the
name of tamkarum" (Sumi tamkcrim lapit). In the "naruqqum contract" of the
trader Elamma (kt 91/k 482) this is the case with no less than four out of thir-
teen investors (good for more than a third of the capital of in all ca. 14 kilo-
grams of gold).45)
41) The word is also used in the meaning "trader (par excellence)" = "Assyrian, con-
trasted with nu'aum, "native Anatolian," in the marriage contract kt n/k 1414:7ff., where a
divorced Anatolian wife is allowed to many "a native or a trader of her choice." A similar
contract, AKT 1 no. 77:7ff., instead has "either a native of an Assyrian" (DUMU ASSur).
Compare the use of "Canaanite" (= Phoenician) for "trader," "merchant" in the Hebrew Old
Testament (Proverbs 3 1:24).
42) Garelli 1977, quote from p. 103.
43) It has been noted that not a single Old Assyrian trader or money-lender calls him-
self tamkarum in the inscription on his seal, while many tamkaru of the Old Babylonian
period did. Seal impression A on the OA text KKS 3b (= p. 164, fig. 7), with the inscrip-
tion "Bala, son of Lani, tamkarum," is no exception; the person mentioned in the inscription
does not occur in the text and the seal (in "Syro-Cappadocian" style) was used by a certain
Su-Anum, who must have acquired it.
44) See Larsen 1967, pp. 49ff. (with older literature), 180f. and Veenhof 1972, p. 352 note
467 and p. 356.
45) At times this (temporary?) anonymity had to be lifted, as happened in the statement
recorded in kt a/k 494 witnessed by two persons: "The two pounds of gold (invested) in the
'money bag' (naruqqum) of P., which have been identified as (belonging to) tamkarum, that
gold belongs to A., A. is the tamkarum." Note also CCT 2, 47b:5ff.: "P. said: the 2 minas
