THE CHIPPED STONE INDUSTRY OF QATAL HtJYUK
105
polished ones from gatal; however, the former have their edges rounded,
whereas most of those from the latter are small greenstone adzes, the sides
are flat, the edges still fairly marked, and the faces asymmetrical. The
lithic industry from the Proto-Chalcolithic level (XXIV) includes some
elements (Fig. 29, bottom row, extreme left and extreme right) which
though they appear at gatal are generalized and problematic, and two
tanged points (Figs. 25 and 28).
The rest of the Early Chalcolithic
(Fig. 29), lacking the finer projectile element, looks rather impoverished,
and should be compared with the lithic industry from Qatal Huyiik West,
which has as yet been merely tested. One should not forget that gatal
Hiiyiik is considerably larger than Mersin and represents an Early Neo-
lithic town in which a more developed industry would be expected. In this
respect the variety of retouched points and the pressure flaked daggers and
lanceheads do not disappoint us.
The " phase A-B " lithic industry of Tell Judeideh in the Amuq
Plain is the rough chronological equivalent of Qatal Hiiyiik (Braidwood
and Braidwood, 1959). Obsidian is only 25 per cent of the total, or a ratio
of slightly more than three to one of chert to obsidian (1,3Io pieces of
chert : 422 pieces of obsidian) in the A-B range, although the same type
of implements are made from both materials. These include javelins,
gravers, sickle blades, borers, scrapers, end scrapers, fabricators, blades
(most not used), small flakes and cores. Only very superficially does the
Judeideh A-B range lithic industry resemble that of gatal Hiiyiik, whereas
a more careful examination reveals considerable differences. The cores
(op. cit., Fig. 30, 9) have an angle of 450 of the striking platform to the
blade scars (a few are said to be 900) and blades have been removed from
only half the perimeter of the core; the cores at gatal (Fig. 10o: 1-4)
have an angle of 900 and are always struck around the whole perimeter.
The javelin heads (Braidwood, op. cit., Fig. 30, 1-3, and Fig. 6o, I-3)
have an expanded basal end, which is completely lacking in the Qatal
examples; also the tanged point classification at gatal includes a wide
variety of arrowheads and lances not found at Judeideh. Short narrow
sickle blades of chert may have their parallels in obsidian at gatal ;
however, they are abundant in the Amuq and barely present at the latter.
The gravers (ibid., Fig. 30o, 8, and Fig. 59, 13, 14) have not been found
at gatal as yet.
The dissimilarities are more striking than the similarities and it is
therefore incomprehensible to the author that one finds " The flint and
obsidian industry of the lower levels of Mersin Hiuyiik, in Cilicia, belongs
to the same culture " (ibid., p. 529) written by Mrs. Crowfoot Payne and
supported by the Braidwoods. The so-called " Syro-Cilician " culture area
I believe to be overdrawn, despite the superficial similarities of the " dark-
faced burnished wares ". A more considered view is found in the Tabbat
al-Hammam publication next to be considered, " Whereas Tabbat al-
Hammam appears to be part of a ceramic family with Mersin and Tarsus,
it is not clear that there are as strong relationships in the stone industries."
(Hole, 1959, P. 177.)

74
ANATOLIAN STUDIES
almost exclusively religious 1 (with the pardonable exception of her son's
brilliant fortune), but the suppression of all secular details here was doubt-
less due to considerations which applied locally to Harran, above all the
need to avoid giving offence to the Medes, who were still in control of
the place.2
A question which the new inscription of Nabonidus himself (H. 2)
leaves in doubt is, when was E-Lul-hul actually restored ? The admonitory
dream appeared in the king's accession-year (VAB. IV, 218, line 16, and
the same is implied by H 2, col. I, i I, though without specific note of
time).
In the Harran inscription the king is enjoined to undertake the
work " quickly ", but this is immediately followed by the mutiny of the
Babylonians, which seemed to frustrate his plan. Again, in reckoning the
desolation of Harran to have lasted for 54 years (see above) both mother
and son date its restoration at the beginning of Nabonidus' reign. There-
after he withdrew to Taima for io years, and according to H. 2, col. III,
the work of rebuilding the temple and restoring the gods was not under-
taken (or at least completed) until after his return to Babylon.
Even
according to the Sippar cylinder already quoted (VAB. IV, 220)
work at
Harran was possible only after the king's third year, when the Median
hold upon Harran was broken by the victory of Cyrus. Yet another indica-
tion is found in the Mother's inscription ; she died in the king's ninth
year, but in col. II,
12 ff., she describes as fulfilled within her own
knowledge (" I saw it myself") the rebuilding of E-hul-bul and Harran,
and the re-introduction of its gods and its cult.
Finally, the Nabonidus-
Cyrus chronicle makes the defeat of Astyages by Cyrus to happen in the
6th year of Nabonidus, and shows that he was engaged in expeditions to
1 Although the reference to her own, and her son's, long service at the court of the
Chaldaean kings insinuates clearly enough the powerful influence which she certainly
exercised there.
2 There seems no need to suppose, as some have done, that Harran was effectively
occupied by the Babylonians in 61o, and that the situation in 556, when it was certainly
controlled by the Medes, had been caused by some subsequent rupture, presumed to have
occurred under Neriglissar.
There is no evidence for this, and it is implied by the
language of Nabonidus himself (extract (e) above) that the Babylonian king had not at
the beginning (nor is he likely to have acquired afterwards) more than a subordinate
control of affairs at Harran.
With this discretion, of adapting the account to the readers, may be compared the
respective descriptions of the famous dream in which the command was given to restore
the cult of Harran.
In the version known hitherto (VAB. IV, 218 f., of which there is
a recent new translation by A. L. Oppenheim, Interpretation of Dreams in the Ancient Near
East, p. 250, cf. p. 203) there is not only the prophecy of the defeat of Astyages and the
Medes (which of course had to be omitted at Harran) but there is an opposite addition
for the benefit of another audience, the Babylonians.
This comprises (i) the introduction
of Marduk as the principal figure (called " Enlil of the gods ") and as the sole speaker,
reducing the Moon-god to a mere spectator, and (2) the injunction to bring the bricks
in the royal chariot, evidently from Babylon, with the notion of authenticity thence
derived. All of this is in stark contrast with the brief, direct and independent command
of the Moon-god related in H. 2, col. I, 12-14.
The artful elaboration of this incident
to the taste of Babylonian readers is as deliberate as it is disingenuous (they had not
been rightly informed, it seems, as H.2 is at pains to explain at its outset).
I am not
sure whether political reporting of this kind remains within " the stylistic requirements
evolved in this specific type of literature ".

mound show that there was an important political centre there also during
the Early Bronze Age, but the lower town came into being somewhat later.
No tablets were found in the houses in the early levels 4 and 3, but it is at
least possible that level 3 represented the beginning of the Assyrian presence
here. In fact, the creation of the lower town, when the houses oflevel 4 were
constructed on virgin soil, could well be understood as a reflection of the
growing commercial importance of the city of Kanesh.
Level 2 contained the richest finds, especially of cuneiform tablets, and a
large number of houses have been excavated from this period. Some I9,ooo
tablets have been found at Kiiltepe since 1948, when Tahsin Ozgii<;:'s excava-
tions began, and the vast majority of those come from level 2. The ca. 4,000
tablets now in museums and collections around the world, those that were
dug up by the villagers and the ones found by Hrozny, should be added to
this figure, for with a few exceptions they too belong to the settlement of
level2. So at this moment more than 23,000 tablets have come from diggings
and excavations at Kiiltepe,
2 and there is no particular reason to believe that
the archives have been exhausted. A few hundred texts stem from the suc-
ceeding period, the one referred to as level I b, whereas the last phase, that
oflevel Ia, which appears to have followed directly upon the destruction by
fire of the houses in level I b, has yielded no tablets or recognisable house
plans at all.
Establishing the stratigraphy and hence the chronology of the lower
town was obviously one of the important first tasks for Ozgii<;: and his team.
Hrozny's plan of the houses he dug was confused, but it seemed clear that it
represented more than one building level. It took a couple of years before
a clear picture emerged, and some amount of disagreement among scholars
was formulated as late as the I950s, but the basic scheme is now generally
accepted. 3 It is also possible to correlate the stratigraphy in the lower town
with the building levels discovered on the mound. The late, large palace
referred to as Warshama's palace is contemporary with level I b in the lower
town, and the lower, round building complex is to be dated to the same
phase as level2. Since, as already indicated, the buildings on the mound were
in general poorly preserved and had been looted and burnt to the ground,
few finds from here can help us establish truly decisive chronological mark-
ers. This situation is further complicated by the fact that so very few of the
texts from the mound have been made available in published form.
The lower town of level 2 consisted of well-built houses of mud brick
on stone foundations. Wood was used extensively. Beams were often placed
horizontally near floor level to serve as support for vertical wooden posts,
and these in turn carried the rafters of the roof. The number of upright posts

zu seinen Lasten, (und) ich bedrängte dich nicht wegen (der Zahlung auch
nur von) 1 Segel Silber! 1045) Mein Vater und Herr bist du, wegen der Tafel
mit dem Urteil der Richter und der Tafel über die Schuld von Amrija und
von dir schrieb ich dir gegen 5-mal folgendermaßen: „Schicke mir die Tafeln,
damit sie mir das Silber, das (ihnen) anvertraut ist, loslassen!" 16-17) Gib
mir eine (Möglichkeit zu einer) Reise! Der Herr soll uns keine Schwierig-
keiten machen! 17-19) Wegen der Tafel über eure Schuld und der Tafel mit
dem Urteil des Richters habe keine Angst! 20-24) Die Strafe sei meine! Sie
werden infolge unseres Rechtsfalls sterben. Und er wird mir eine Tafel ge-
ben, daß er auf dich und deine Söhne nicht zurückkommen wird. 24-28) Mein
Vater und Herr bist du, sobald du meine Tafel hörst, schicke die Tafeln,
damit der Herr mich bei Beginn meiner Reise nicht mit Vorwürfen wegen
der Tafeln überschüttet! 28-30) Der Gott hat mich verworfen, und ich wurde
vor dir bloßgestellt. Wegen des Hannanärum bedränge ich dich. 31-36) Lege
dort eine Liste für mich über das, was Ennum-Assur dir brachte, an, ziehe
alles Silber, das ich (bereits) nahm, und alles Silber, das ich auf deinen Na-
men zahlte, ab und schicke mir den Rest meines Silbers mit Ennum-Assur.
36-38) Mein Vater, <mein Herr> bist du, setz dich nicht selbst unter Druck
und setz auch mich nicht unter Druck! 38-45) Mein Vater, mein Herr bist
du, wenn ich dich wirklich wieder wegen des Hannanärum infolge verleum-
derischen Geredes von bösen Menschen mit Vorwürfen überschütte, wenn
ich wirklich für dich Unrecht und Gemeinheiten habe, was kann ich dann
sagen? 45-48) Mein Bruder und Herr bist du, kläre dort in Vertretung von
dir (selbst) und mir und schicke mir dann jeden einzelnen Segel Silber, der
übrig bleibt. 48-5i) Tafeln und Aufzeichnungen habe ich nicht in der Hand,
und ich will nicht mit bösem Herzen zur Stadt reisen. Tu (also), was gut
ist.

APPENDIX C
study, but for our purposes we may see the end of the EB II phase (or to
be less precise, the end of the Early Bronze Age itself), as a period during
which the old Anatolian city-state system is subjected to the impact of
new populations and social forms. Let us consider the effect of this
impact upon the region of Central Anatolia.
Tahsin Özgüç points out that "In the Kayseri Plain there are signs of
violent burning in various levels belonging to the late and middle phases
of the Early Bronze Age".3' Kültepe [EB IIIa] and Alaca Hüyük [Stratum
5] are two such important cities which were affected. About this cata-
strophe and these sites Professor Goetze writes :
Alaca Hüyük ended in a catastrophe, and this catastrophe has left its mark
wherever excavations have been conducted. It is this break, ushering in the
Middle Bronze Age, with which the Hittite period begins. Much as it took
over from the preceding period — in fact, it is so much that the ethnic change
would hardly be recognizable to the archaeologist — nevertheless, the texts
which fortunately begin to become available at this point — first in Kültepe
and then in Boğazköy — teach us that the Hittites, or at least close relatives
of the Hittites, were present in Anatolia at the beginning of the Middle Bronze
Age.32
Although the weight of scholarly opinion supports this interpretation
Özgüç denies that the Hittites were responsible for bringing the rich EB
culture to an end. A treatment of his reasoning will allow a convenient
transition to a discussion of the appearance of Old Assyrian traders in
Anatolia.33
Özgüç believes that the Hittites were a minority when they came to
Anatolia and remained so for a period of time afterward. On the other
hand, the princes of North and Central Anatolia "lived in rich and forti-
fied cities" and "were experienced in the use of all kind of weapons".34
Therefore these princes "cannot have fallen victims to Hittite invaders
who were of a lower level of civilization and small in numbers".35
Further, "The Hittites were too insignificant to be responsible for the
simultaneous and ubiquitous destruction and burning of these cities".36
Such statements appear to show little if any familiarity with the
realities of ancient Near Eastern history. One need only point to Amorite
successes over substantial Sumerian-controlled cities or Israelite successes
over strong Canaanite cities of Palestine to dismiss the hypothesis that

Mesopotamian MIs Pi Ritual
tradition of the series which followed the Babylonian tradition in placing that incanta­
tion first and which placed the incantation tablets before the ritual text might then des­
ignate the whole series by this opening line en an-na nf-bi-ta tu-ud-da-a. Unfor­
tunately the colophon on the BR gives no series title.
Neo-Assyrian letters and historical texts refer to the performance of KA.LUij.U.DA
but not LUij KA. Although we do not find KA.LUij.U.DA in the colophon of any tablet at
present, many of the incantations edited here (particularly in Incantation Tablet 1/2)
have the rubric ka-inim-ma ... dingir-ra ka-lub-u-da-kam.
Provisionally we have to conclude that there is evidence for the material which
we edit here having circulated under two different names, LUij KA (at Assur, Nineveh,
and Sippar?) and en an-na ni-bi-ta tu-ud-da-a (at Nimrud, Sultantepe, and per­
haps Babylon). As will be discussed below, even these two titles may designate more
than one order of the ritual and incantations. Bottero (1985: 68) suggests that the two
rituals LUij KA and KA.LUij.U.DA listed in KAR 44 are the same. We are agnostic on
this point, but suggest as an alternative that some of the incantations which include ka­
lub-u-da in their rubrics (particularly those represented by K 351 1 [Tablet 1/2 Text
CD might have circulated as a separate collection for use on those many occasions
when something other than a divine image had its mouth washed. Compare the way in
which the prayers and incantations which have respectively the runrics ka-inim-ma
su-iI͹la-kam and ka-inim-ma udug-bul-a-kam are collectively known as SU-illa
and Udug-bul.
Despite our uncertainty on these various points throughout the following pages
we refer to our texts under the collective title of Mis Pi. 19
Mouth-Washing20
Ceremonial washings of the mouth were not restricted to the Mis Pi ritual. There are
numerous references to mouth-washing outside our ritual.
1.
The procedure could be used in various royal rituals involving either the king or
his royal insignia.21 In 4 R2 17 rev. 25, in an incantation recited in the fourth house
of the Bit Rimki (Laessee 1955: 51; Seux 1976: 393), the king says mu-us-su pi­
ia, "my mouth is washed." In the fifth house of Bit Rimki, the king spits on a
statue and then washes his mouth with water and beer (Laessee 1955: 30 line 25;
Hurowitz 1989: 53-54).
2.
Animals and sacred objects also could have their "mouths" washed. The mouth of
a tukkanu, a leather bag used for oracles, is washed in BBR 21 :30 and the dupli­
cate BBR 74:29-30 (enilma pi tukanni JlibiirO imessu "when the biirO-priest
washes the mouth of a leather bag,,)22. In the ritual for covering the JiJjssu-drum
with the bull's hide (RAcc 1921: 1-33; Boden 1998: 20 n 37; new treatment in
19 We do not have a title for the royal ritual originally published by G. Meier under the title "Mund­
waschung" (Meier 1937/9; see now BerJejung 1996), nor is it apparent at present whether this text is
among those listed in KAR 44.
20 See Berlejung 1998: 182-185; the "mouth washing" procedure was far more extensively used than in
just our particular ritual. Also see Seux 1976: 269. The divine statues (as a whole) were regularly
washed, but the verb here is not mesO but ramaku (Laess0e 1955: 16 n 20). This is the ritual nanaku
referred to in the Assyrian month name nanak Assur sa kiniitim.
21 See Appendix B on Meier's "royal ritual".
22 See BBR 11 :30 e-nu-ma KA KUS.oUG.GAN IUijAL LUij-Ii [ ].

DAS PALASTGESCHÄFT IN DER ALTBABYLONISCHEN ZEIT 	155
selbst bezeichnen eine bestimmte Gruppe solcher Entrepreneure als "Ertragsbringer" (nc- i
biltim).
Die Wahl des Terminus Entrepreneur scheint mir gerechtfertig im Lichte der
klassischen wirtschaftshistorischen Definition, die auf die Verwendung des Wortes in
historischem Bezug zum französischen Merkantilismus zurückgeht, und den der Ökonom
I.M. Kirzner folgendermaßen defmiert:
" ... By the seventeenth century the term was used to designate the risk bearer,
typically 'a person who entered into a contractual relationship with the government
for the performance of a service or the supply of goods. The price at which the
contract was valued was fixed and the entrepreneurs bore the risks of profit and loss
from the bargain'."7
Die typischen Elemente eines Palastgeschäftes sind:
1.Der Palast überträgt die Durchführung wi rtschaftlicher Aktivitäten einem En trepreneur.
2. Der Entrepreneur trägt das Risiko.
3. Das Palastgeschäft wird vertraglich, d. h. per Rechtsurkunde, vereinbart und enthält die
folgenden, wichtigen Stipulationen:
4. Diese regeln:
a) die Leistungspflicht des Entrepreneurs, die
b) entweder fest nach Menge etc. fixiert ist, oder proportional a p riori vereinbart ist und
im Leistungsfall durch Schätzung (šukunnûm) ermittelt wird.
Aber Kirzner betont auch, daß letztendlich der Staat das Risiko übernahm, wenn der
Entrepreneur in Schwierigkeiten geriet, denn der Staat war auf die fortdauernden Dienste
des Entrepreneurs angewiesen.
2. Die Hauptformen des Palastgeschäftes
Drei hauptsächliche Formen des Palastgeschäftes lassen sich unterscheiden:
- Palastgeschäfte, die auf landwirtschaftlicher Produktion oder Viehhaltung beruhen,
- Palastgeschäfte, in denen es um das Ausbeuten natürlicher Ressourcen geht, und
schließlich
- Palastgeschäfte, deren Aufgabe im Bereich der Dienstleistung liegen, die teilweise mit
den beiden zuvor genannten Formen von Palastgeschäften verbunden sein können.
Zur ersten Gruppe gehört der Getreideanbau, die Dattelpalmkultivation und der
Anbau von sonstigen Feld- und Gartenprodukten, wie Ölsaaten, Zwiebeln usw. Bei der
Herdenhaltung geht es zunächst um die Wollerzeugung und in zweiter Linie um das
Bereitstellen von Schlachtvieh. Bei der Rinderhaltung steht die Versorgung institutioneller
Haushalte mit Arbeitstieren im Vordergrund, während die Produktion von
Milchprodukten eine zweitrangige Rolle gespielt zu haben scheint
Unter den Palastgeschäften, deren Zweck auf die Ausbeutung natürlicher Ressourcen
gerichtet ist, ist der Fisch- und Vogelfang in den Kanälen und Horen (Hôrs) am besten

Chicago 1979, 39.

concluded between the two; 1121 and 2) a later lawsuit, where the party of Sueyya
contested a point in the previous settlement, and where Ennam-Assur was confronted by
the attorney of Piisu-ken's estate and a certain Ikun-piya. 1122 Although the latter stage is
the one of interest in the present context, a few elements of the sworn agreement made
during the early stage need to be mentioned.
The first settlement concerned a large number of economic bonds between the close
associates Ennam-Assur and Sueyya, which also involved a certain Kuziya. Among other
things we learn: 1) Sueyya owed more than llh talent of silver to Ennam-Assur that had
been collected by Ennam-Assur; 2) Sueyya was entitled to collect at least 35 minas of
silver from Ennam-Assur; 3) Sueyya had served as guarantor for a debt worth 50 minas
of silver owed by a Kuziya to Ennam-Assur. Moreover, two important things happened
upon the conclusion of the sworn agreement:
After we made them swear the oath of the city and Ennam-Assur gave Sueyya a tablet
(stating) that he (i.e. Ennam-Assur) will not raise any claims on any of these issues,
Ennam-Assur said:
"Will Sueyya not give me a tablet stating that he will not raise claims against me on any of
these issues?"
The representative of Sueyya, U~ur-sa-Assur, said:
"He will not raise a claim against you. We did not bring the 40 minas of silver (in) the two
containers under the seal of Sueyya into your house."
Text 78 - KUG 13 (EL 332) 37-47: Excerpt of the sworn agreement between Ennam-
Assur and the party of Sueyya. 1123
Upon the conclusion of the first settlement Ennam-Assur issued a legal document to
Sueyya stating that he would not raise claims against Sueyya in the future. With Eisser
and Lewy, a copy of the document in question is possibly the one preserved as CCT 1, 9a
(EL 333), which concerns the termination of Sueyya's duty as guarantor for the debt of
50 minas of silver. In return Ennam-Assur requested that Sueyya signed a tablet stating
that he also would refrain from future claims on these matters. We do not know whether
he obtained a tablet of this kind, but at least Ennam-Assur was given oral confirmation by
U~ur-sa-Assur, Sueyya's representative, namely that Sueyya would not raise future
claims. But U~ur-sa-Assur also added that Sueyya's party "did not bring 40 minas of
silver into Ennam-Assur's house". The added statement is crucial for the understanding
of the subsequent conflict that arose slightly later, as it concerns the ownership of exactly
this sum of money. The phrase used by U~ur-sa-Assur, "to bring something into the

Der Text macht der Auslegung erhebliche Schwierigkeiten; es kann
sich daher bei den folgenden Ausführungen nur um einen Erklärungsversuch
handeln. Auffällig ist schon, daß der Name des Schuldners, der nach Z. 16
bis 23 insgesamt 16 Minen 17 Segel in 3 Raten darzuwägen hat, nicht ange-
geben ist. Der eigentliche Urkundentext scheint nur bis Z. 32a zu reichen;
es folgt eine Notiz (Z. 32b-39) oder, falls in Z. 39 ein Zeugenname zu er-
gänzen ist (vgl. die Anm. zu Z. 39 der Übersetzung), eine weitere Urkunde, in
welch letzterem FalI es sich bei dem ganzen Text um eine Sammelurkunde
handeln würde (vgl. dazu Vorbemerkung v0r Nr. 224). Die Weglassung des
Schuldnernamens und die Hinzufügung der Notiz machen es wahrscheinlich,
daß es sich bei dem Text nicht um eine Originalurkunde, sondern um eine
auszugsweise Abschrift einer im Original vollständigen Urkunde handelt, die
sich der Schuldner für seine eigenen Zwecke angefertigt und durch die Notiz
ergänzt hat.
Die Abschrift (Z. 1-320) macht zunächst 11 Personen namhaft, denen
der Schuldner verschieden hohe Silberbeträge schuldet. Der Schuldner hat
an 3 aufeinander folgenden Festen des Nibas je 1/3 seiner Schuld darzu-
wägen. Die Schuld ist nach Z. 24-27 durch ein dem „Palast" (jur. Er-
läuterung § 2) zustehendes Zugriffsrecht an dem Vermögen des Schuldners
gesichert; der Palast scheint eine Art Treuhänder fur die Gläubiger ge-
wesen zu sein (vgl. dazu auch De Clercq [oben Nr. 209] Z. 5-13). Die
einheitliche Zusammenfassung der verschiedenen Schuldsummen und die dem
Palast zustehende Zugriffsmöglichkeit auf die Aktiva des Schuldners lassen
den Schluß zu, daß es sich um einen Vergleich des (überschuldeten oder
zahlungsunfähigen?) Schuldners mit seinen verschiedenen Gläubigern handelt;
dazu paßt auch die ganz verschiedene Höhe der Einzelansprüche, die sich
aus der Umrechnung der Gläubigerforderungen auf einen Stichtag zwanglos
erklären lassen könnte. Der Palast scheint bei diesem Vergleichsverfahren
mitgewirkt zu haben, wie aus seinem Zugriffsrecht hervorgeht. Vielleicht
hatte er auch die Ratenzahlungen des Schuldners in Empfang zu nehmen
und auf die verschiedenen Gläubiger zu verteilen, da über diese Frage nichts
bestimmt ist. Ob und in wieweit die Forderungen der Gläubiger bei der
anteilsmäßigen Berechnung nach Z. 1-15 herabgesetzt worden sind, steht
dahin. Der Vergleich selbst scheint in der in Z. 33 erwähnten „Tafel des

405
negative markers (barbarisms).27 This assumption is needed in order to explain why the
forms with –n- continued to be the norm up to the very end of the Hittite Empire, even
though the n-less forms appear as early as Middle Hittite. The English –in’ forms
represent a good example of a low class/ poor education marker that has been remaining
a stable linguistic variable for the last two centuries, both in England and in the United
States. The widespread prejudice against the cross-dialectal change –ing > -in’ continues
to inhibit the penetration of the natively transmitted suffix -in’ into the formal, educated
speech.

6.2.4 Was New Hittite a Dead Language? It is time to sum up our provisional
conclusions. Some of the New Hittite changes, suggested in the previous literature, have
received an analogical or graphic explanation. What remains is the change /i/ > /e/ that
was spreading through lexical diffusion throughout the New Hittite period, and the
stigmatized development [an] > [ã] that existed on the margins of the New Hittite
written culture. These two features provide solid evidence for the fact that Hittite
continued to be spoken on certain occasions in the Empire period, and not merely used
as a tool for the composition of written texts. It remains to be seen whether these data
are sufficient to claim that Hittite continued to be natively transmitted into the thirteenth
century BC.

27 I am using here the term marker in a sense given to it by William Labov (as opposed to
indicator). For the concise definitions of indicator, marker, and stereotype with reference to linguistic
variables see Wardhaugh 1996: 140.

THE HISTORICAL GEOGRAPHY OF ANATOLIA AND THE TRANSITION
FROM THE KARUM-PERIOD TO THE EARLY HITTITE EMPIRE
Massimo Forlanini
The last years have brought new evidence about the Old Assyrian Colony Period in
Anatolia (from the end of the twentieth to the end of the eighteenth century BC): a
considerable number of cuneiform tablets from the Turkish excavations of Kültepe has
been published recently, among them documents of new types, like the History of Sargon,
the lit-nu-lists (presently limited to the Karam Level II period), and several treaties
ô between the merchants' organisation and local rulers.' This new evidence broadens the
horizon of our understanding of the history and chronology of the period; besides, the
discovery of another eponym list (Kt 01/k 287) extending well into the lb Period and a
w letter (Kt 01/k 217) mentioning king Hurmeli of I-Jarsamna and his relations with Aur,
Zalpa and king am3i-Adad, have been announced by C. Günbatti. 2 In recent years, there
has been intense research on different topics trying to organize the huge quantity of
available data. This has resulted in a substantial improvement in our understanding of the
Old Assyrian period. 3 However, new data about itineraries of the Anatolian circuit,
O
0 	 gleaned from the texts recently published, do not seem to improve or change dramatically
our knowledge and a reconstruction of the Anatolian geography still depends heavily on
Hittite documents.
.5
Students of the Old Assyrian period must cope with difficult linguistic and
philological problems and intricate researches concerning prosopography, chronology and
economic relations; moreover, since the writing, the language and the nature of
1 Editions of the "History of Sargon" found in Kültepe: Günbatti 1998, Van de Mieroop
2000, Dercksen 2006. For research on the relative chronology of the Old Assyrian Period and the
sequence of the eponyms of the period Kültepe Kamm Level II, see Veenhof 2003 and Kryszat
x 2004; Kryszat starts from this sequence in order to investigate the historical position of merchants
and companies. Michel (2002) has proposed a new "lowered middle chronology" for the absolute
chronology, using dendrochronological evidence from Acemhöyük. Günbatti (2004) has published
the fragmentary treaties which the Assyrians concluded with Kane§ (Kt 00/k 6) and tlal bum (Kt
00/k 10).
2 Cf. Günbatti 2005: 450, and his paper at the VI. Internationales Colloquium der Deutschen
Orient-Gesellschaft ("Hattu:;a-Bogazkby. Das Hethiterreich im Spannungsfeld des Alten
Orients"), Würzburg, 22-24 March, 2006. With the announced extension of the eponym list the
total number of year-names will be 252, covering the Ib Period until 1723 BC.
3 See note 1 above. Students are particularly indebted to I.G. Dercksen for his reference
works on the Old Assyrian copper trade (1996) and institutions (2004), and to C. Michel for her
comprehensive updated treatment of the Old Assyrian period, with the translation of 400 texts
(2001, here referred as CMK followed by text number) and for her Old Assyrian Bibliography
(2003), an indispensable tool for any future research. A fundamental starting point for further
historical and geographical research on the eighteenth century is offered by Dercksen (2001).

2.6 Summary
This chapter has offered some background of the political and economic structure of
early complex societies in Western Asia and introduced a landscape perspective with
which we can frame the study of political, material and social trajectories. A key point
to reiterate is that exclusive adoption of a pre-constituted theoretical framework
privileging distinctions or similarities between modern and pre-modern societies is
unhelpful for understanding political and economic processes in Western Asia in the
Bronze Age. In addition, the patchy characteristics of both archaeological and textual
evidence do not offer an unequivocal picture of past early complex-societies, and we
have to try to integrate those two kinds of dataset if we want to detect the structure
and the spatial scale of past political and economic landscapes. Thus, in the present
dissertation two directions of enquiry seem to be necessary. First, the available
archaeological data must be reviewed with the aim of detecting what political,
economic and/or social factors could have caused the spread of specific types of
material culture and social practices (e.g. pottery, seals, metrology, architecture,
burials, urban layout, etc.). Second, the analysis of written sources should provide a
picture unveiling the diachronic and spatial development of early polities and their
interactions both on local and inter-regional scale. In both cases computational, spatial
modelling can also be of assistance. Undoubtedly, political landscapes in Upper
Mesopotamia and Central Anatolia in the Middle Bronze Age were affected by radical
shifts between political systems (e.g. from peer-polity system of city-states to
territorial states) that consequently acted at different spatial scales and with different
degrees of pervasiveness and authority. Chapter 4 and 5 will address those issues,
while Chapter 6 will try to trace modalities and scales of interaction between various
political and trade agencies.

1. 24: the stress on all of the envoys from the City indicates that we have to do with more
than the two who are otherwise attested.
I. 33: the term tiri ekallim is not otherwise attested in Old Assyrian. I owe my understanding

Comment:
The two letters nos. 109 and 110 both deal in detail with the complications arising out of
Salim-Assur's dealings in copper and wool in Burushaddum, where he was confronted with
the important local official called Usinalam. This version is addressed to a group of persons
in Kanesh who represent the interests of Salim-Assur there, and it is noteworthy that none of
his sons are among them. We hear that a delegation from Burushaddum has arrived at
Kanesh with letters addressed to the colony. These letters set out the details of an affair that
had ended up with Abu-salim owing a very large amount of copper to Usinalam, and insist
that he or his master, Salim-Assur, must come to Burushaddum to pay the debt. Salim-Assur
is of course writing from Burushaddum, where he went in fetters. He paid a large amount of
copper to cover his slave's debt, but Usinalam was still not satisfied and handed him over to
the palace officials for a further debt. The exact background for these actions is not clear, nor
does it appear who was the debtor in this latter case. Salim-Assur offered Usinalam certain
that the Anatolian official had apparently given to him earlier, but
Usinalam refers to certain other tablets according to which Salim-Assur would have to pay.
In the duplicate no. 110 Salim-Assur seems to say to Usinalam that these documents are now
his, but that he will give them anyway. And instead of the reference to "those other tablets"
Usinalam in that text is speaking of "his sakkanakku". Although several essential details in
the procedure remain obscure, it comes across clearly that Salim-Assur desperately needs as-
sistance, and that he wishes the entire affair dealt with by the City assembly in Assur. The
duplicate text addressed to the authorities in Kanesh has a number of more lines in badly
broken passages. See photo 41.

48
HAKAN EROL
Assur-rabi, Baziya, Ennanum, Enna-Suen, Iddin-abum, Iliya, Kudadum, Kururu and U~ur-sa-
Assur are prominent. Assur-bani and Baziya generally acted as transporters. It seems none of the
individuals in the list had close kinship relations with the family.
The name of Baziya occurs in about twenty documents, and in three of them (no. 27: 34;
Kt 92/k 550: 3; 554a: 4) he is recorded as the son of Asqudum. Based on data found in KTS 1,
55b, K. R. Veenhof assumed that Baziya took over his father Asqudum's 121 duty of commercial
representation of the palace in Assur. 122 Even though it is not certain that they are the same per-
sons attested commonly in our archive, Ali-ahum, according to the unpublished Kt 93/k 363,
and Ennam-Anum, according to the legal documents (Kt 92/k 293, 328, 332, 336, 426, 543a/b)
regarding the case between Assur-imitfi and Assur-na~ir invested money in the naruqqum partner-
ship carried by Su-Istar.
Dadaya and Suen-damiq informed Su-Istar in their letter (no. 70) from Assur, that the in-
vestors (ummeanf:t) of Su-Istar held them back and tried to seize their silver,which the investors
claimed belonged to Su-Istar. His term for the loan was up, and they had brought it secretly to
Assur. Dadaya and Suen-damiq declared that the silver did not belong to Su-Istar, but that it was
their own credit (qptum). According to letter no. 71, which deals with the same subject, it is clear
that those ummeanu of Su-Istar were in fact Assur-imitfi son of Ataya and an anonymous person
described as the !tmum of the god A dad. Those people could have either invested in the naruqqum
of Su-Istar or given him a loan with interest.
Anonymous "traders" (tamkarum) occur in about twenty documents. It is possible that Su-
Istar himself was meant in some of them. However, in most cases, the "trader" was related to
Su-Istar's business ventures, and seems to be in a higher position than him. For instance, in a
letter (no. 27) from Assur-rabi, Sarrum-Suen and Baziya to Su-Istar and tamkarum, the transport
of a huge amount of tin belonging to both of them is mentioned. In his letter to Su-Istar, La-qep
demanded one or two donkeys before the winter arrives, so that tamkarum would not become
angry at him. As it is well known, the term tamkarum has different meanings in the Old Assyrian
texts, such as the chair of a firm in Anatolia, or an itinerant merchant of merchandise in Anatolia,
or even a commercial agent or investor. Accordingly, it is usually not possible to ascertain who
the real persons were referred to as tamkarum.
The Commercial Merchandise
The main goods traded by the family of Su-Istar were the tin and varied textiles brought
from Assur, as is characteristic of the Old Assyrian trade network. The goods that were brought
into Kanis by the family seem to have gone on from there primarily to the cities located north of
Kanis, and sold to the local palaces there in exchange for copper. Then the acquired copper was
converted to silver in other cities. Beyond for these main goods, at the least some other merchan-
dise also occurs as commercial goods in the texts: wool (No. 78, 116, 128; Kt 92/k 418, 440, 442,
685), leather (Kt 92/k 442), bronze (No. 9, 87; Kt 92/k 446) and especially amutum/asium (iron).

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
inscription is the foundation document (? XXXVIII ff.). In spite of this reticence
about his exact locus standi in Adana, Azatiwatas does definitely rank himself
with kings (?? XVII-XVIII, XXVI-XXVII, LII, LIX-LXIV).
He presents
himself as a prince, perhaps of the Adana ruling house of Muksas, who was raised
by a king of Adana to the rulership of a district lying north-east of the plain of
Adana and including Azatiwataya-Karatepe.
Subsequent to the reign of his
lord, Azatiwatas was in a position to confer many benefits on Adana and in fact
ensured the succession of his lord's family there, thus showing himself to be
acting as regent over Adana and guardian to the house of Muksas. So much may
be safely accepted from an examination of the text itself.
Attempts to date the monuments of Karatepe have been based both on
stylistic criteria of the sculptures and on historical considerations. Among the
latter, one important point is the identification of the KARATEPE hieroglyphic
name Awarikus with the Urikki (and variants) attested in cuneiform sources of
Tiglath-pileser III (for 738 and 732 B.C.)3 and Sargon (for 710-9 B.C.),4 and the
possible, though not established, supposition that both the hieroglyphic and the
cuneiform references are to the same individual.
If Awarikus was not the same as Urikki but a homonymous forebear on the
throne of Adana, he could hardly have reigned as a contemporary of Shalmaneser
III, for whom other contemporaries in Que are attested.5 He could thus have
reigned either before Shalmaneser III or between Shalmaneser III and Tiglath-
pileser III, periods for which we have no historical information about Que.6 The
KARATEPE inscription could be fitted without difficulty, but equally without
corroboration, into either of these two historically blank periods. Arguments
for one or the other can only rest on alleged stylistic criteria.7
If, however, Awarikus is to be identified with Urikki - and the discussion
which follows will be based on this agsumption -- the range of possible dates for
Azatiwatas is strictly limited.
Assyrian relations with Que from 745 B.C. are
documented, though sparsely. Urikki was king of Que in 738 and 732 B.C., and
was still alive in 710-9 B.C.8 though not necessarily on the throne, since by that
date an Assyrian governor of Que is attested. It is not clear at what date before
710 Que became an Assyrian province,9 nor how Urikki survived the take-over.
This latest date for Urikki, which has only been established comparatively recent-
ly, does, however, have the effect of lowering the hypothetical date of Azatiwatas
by over twenty years.
The position of Que within the Assyrian Empire presumably lasted until 705
when Sargon met his death in battle, but it seems likely that thereupon Que

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
To summarize the initial thinking, late Roman Asia Minor was a wealthy
urban society which survived up to the beginning of the seventh century. In the
crisis of the twenty-five year war with Persia and the rise of Islam, the
Byzantine empire lost its wealthiest provinces in Syria, Palestine and above all
Egypt, and what followed was a dour struggle to preserve what remained. In
the first phase of Byzantine-Arab warfare, which lasted up to the beginning of
the eighth century, the Muslim goal was the capture of Constantinople and
fighting concentrated either on the imperial capital itself or on the coastlands of
western Asia Minor. It had become clear already by the mid-seventh century
that Constantinople was too far away and too well defended to be taken by an
army coming direct from Syria whether by land or sea. To capture the city
would require blockade as much as assault, and to achieve that the Arabs tried
to conquer the coastlands of western Asia Minor as an ideal winter base from
which to maintain the pressure. References in Byzantine, Syriac and Arabic
sources to the importance of the fortresses at Pergamon, Ephesos and Sardis
are confirmed by the physical evidence of substantial new defences, testifying to
a battle for control of this crucial region. After the failure of the 716-18 siege
Arab strategy switched to one of regular raids into Byzantine territory, aiming
to amass booty and win ideological credit as true warriors of the djihad rather
than in expectation of the empire's conquest in the near future. The Byzantines
gradually responded to this new threat by developing a strategy of mobile
counter-raiding warfare-usefully
described in a tenth-century handbook of
practical advice-and by what amounted to a policy of defence in depth. The
Arabs were allowed access to the central Anatolian plateau where the rural
population had been evacuated in advance. The columns of enemy raiders were
shadowed, picked off when they tried to spread out for plunder and forage,
and finally it was hoped trapped in the passes as they tried to cross back into
northern Syria, tired and laden with booty. Only if Arab armies tried to push
on beyond the ring of mountains which surround the plateau would they face
major fortresses, such as Ankara or Ktitahya, and eventually field armies that
would bring them to battle. It was for example at Afyon, on the western rim of
the plateau, that the Arabs suffered a serious defeat at the battle of Akrofnos
in 742. Western Asia Minor in this period was generally a well-protected region
far from any danger from the east.
From the later ninth century onwards, as the Abbasid caliphate began to
break up into less formidable political units, the tide of war turned in favour of
the Byzantines, and imperial forces moved on to the offensive, pushing far into
the Transcaucasus, Syria and Mesopotamia. The following period of pax
Byzantina was brought to an end by the invasions of the Turks. After the battle
of Malazgirt in 1071 imperial control over Asia Minor collapsed, and when the
situation had eventually stabilised in the early twelfth century only about a
third of Asia Minor remained in Byzantine hands. Western Asia Minor was no
longer a secure province, deep in the interior of the empire, but a frontier zone,
exposed to Turkish attack. The collapse of imperial authority in the last quar-
ter of the twelfth century which culminated in the fall of Constantinople to the
Latins in 1204 led to further losses in Asia Minor, but the frontier stabilised
again as an empire-in-exile under the Laskarid dynasty established itself with its
centre in the Izmir region. Laskarid success however led to the recovery of
Constantinople in 1261 and a consequent change in priorities. By the end of the
thirteenth century, western Asia Minor was effectively, and now permanently,
in Turkish hands.

From the waklum to the Kanesh colony:
The City assembly has passed a verdict in
front of the two great pegs in the sacred
precinct stating that Iddin-Suen son of
Ibaya may hire an attorney, and ...
Salim-Assur son of Issu-arik ...
The eponymy of IlSu-rabi son of Baziya.
Su-Kubum son of Assur-idi is my attorney.
This letter from the king in Assur, in which he relays a decision taken by the City assembly,
is dated to the year KEL 88. It is presumably the verdict that is being revoked in the preced-
ing letter. The letter is written during the reign of Sargon I, and the hand seems very similar
to POAT 18a+ b which carries the seal of Sargon. Cf. Kryszat 2004 for the chronology of the
royal letters. Despite the sealing, Kryszat places POA T 18 in the very beginning of the reign
of Puzur-Assur II. See photo 12.

and the museum director, who is the only person who has this information, was
unexpectedly away in Europe at the time of our visit. We hope to be able to
consult with him and with his predecessor in order to have more accurate
information on this area. We did visit a number of these village sites; in the
majority of cases the mosaics were unexcavated and only partially visible, but
enough for the conservator to make an assessment. In a small number of cases
the mosaics had been destroyed as recently as two or three years ago, as they were
"in the way" of a farmer's field. In two other cases reports of mosaics could not
be followed up as the roads were impassable.
Following reports of mosaics north of Feke, and Saimbeyli which we visited
last year, we went to the area of §ar and there found four more mosaics in village
houses. In one case, where there are two mosaics which are exposed, they are
suffering greatly, especially in the room used as a stable. In the other two
houses the mosaics are under at least 1 m. of earth and hence are well protected.
We have been unable to ascertain whether any of these are the mosaics briefly
mentioned by R. Harper in Anatolian Studies XVIII (1968) p. 135. There were
reports of another mosaic 5 km. away but the road was too bad for us to try to
find it.
On the return to Adana we stopped again at Feke, a site we visited last
year, in order to repeat some of the photographs of last season which had not
turned out well. There we discovered that a mosaic inscription which we had
recorded had almost completely disappeared in the few intervening months, thus
reinforcing the urgency of this project.
The second half of June and the month of July I spent working at Anamur.
We found one totally new mosaic floor, a church, uncovered more of the known
mosaics on two other floors, i.e. Point Anamur and the small baths, and found
several more fragments of mosaic in the Necropolis Church.
This season a great many problems interfered with a smooth progression
of the work planned. In spite of the delay we were able to accomplish a great
deal as described above.
Although we were forced to work under great
pressure the results were very gratifying. Even with only minimal study of our
findings, important information is becoming clear. In the past Antioch has
always been considered the centre for mosaics in this part of the Roman
Empire, probably because they were the only large group collected and
published.
However, the Antioch group, although very important, can no
longer be considered to be the chief and sole centre and stimulus for Asia Minor
mosaics. Other major centres are emerging whose repertoire is quite distinct
from that of Antioch. By the time the survey is completed and the material
synthesized, we should be able to pin-point these centres more precisely.
Reports on this and past seasons' work were presented to colleagues in
December at the Briarcliff Conference on Greek, Roman and Byzantine
Studies, and at the annual meetings of the American Institute of Archaeology.
Published accounts have appeared in the American Journal of Archaeology,
Tirk Arkeoloji Dergisi and Anatolian Studies.
The continued co-operation
and participation of colleagues in the field are solicited and encouraged.
Future Proposals
Since a lot of information has been gathered in the last two field trips, it
seems best not to have a field trip in 1977 but rather to begin synthesizing the
information we already have, putting it into the form it will have for the
publication and completing the background research on this amount. For 1978

after the exceptional standard of the masonry, suggesting that this must have been
a wealthy town, of which only the acropolis remains to this day. It is presumably to
this site that Lynch refers when he mentions " the city of Hayk, in the Hayotz-dzor,
south-east of Van ". It is possible that this is the site of the fortress of Sardurihurda.
From the sketch-plan
certain important
features can be seen :-
(I) At either extremity of this long fortress is a ditch cut through the rock ridge,
for defence : the N.E. ditch is 8 m. in width and maximum depth; the S.W. ditch
is slightly narrower ; originally both must have been considerably deeper, before
silting. (2) Access to the fortress was by a staircase-the steps of which are now
buried-cut through the rock, 2 m. wide, with a smooth, vertical rock face on
both sides, so that it could easily be blocked in time of siege. (3) Within the fortress
are two vertical shafts (2 XI -5 m. and 4 m. apart), which may have been cisterns ;
they have rounded corners and a flat rock-cut platform surrounds them. (4) On
its S.W. side the North-East Kale has the rock cut almost vertically along the line
of the wall, which was thus heightened, with a flat rock platform just beneath.
This N.E. Kale has stone buildings inside its walls.
(5) The purpose of the
enclosure-quarried out of the rock and walled in on the open N.W. side-is not
clear. The enclosure wall is cyclopean, with characteristic batter, and about 4 m.
thick : in one part it is preserved ten courses (over 4 m.) high. It has three towers
each projecting i m., being 5 m. wide and 7 "5
m. apart.
(6) The main fortified
area was to the south-west, connected to the N.E. Kaei by parallel screen-walls.

Area D Trenches 8 and 10
Suspecting that the area to the south of trench D5 may contain more remains of
Ubaid Structure 1, we began a 5 by 10 meter excavation unit (designated trench D8) south
of trench D5 during the 2004 field. Since trench D8 began at the same level on the slope of
the mound as trench D5, we knew that it will take at least two field seasons to reach the
Ubaid levels. (These layers lie some 2.5 meters below ground surface on the uphill side of
the trench). Much to our delight, we reached two phases of extensive and well-preserved
Ubaid architecture in trench D8 at the beginning of the 2005 field season. This discovery
prompted us to open another trench (D10) just south of trench D8 (figures 3 and 4).
The results from these trenches are truly impressive. The first architecture we
encountered was slightly higher in elevation than the remains of Ubaid Structure 1 in
trench D5 (described above). Ceramic analysis nevertheless confirmed that these remains
belong to the Late Northern Ubaid cultural complex. This architecture consisted of two
groups of mud brick walls running roughly north-south and east-west (figure 5, this
architecture is hereafter referred to as Ubaid Structure 2). These walls intersected at
roughly right angles forming a series of small square or rectangular chambers or cells
measuring between 1 and 1.5 meters in width. In the north half of trench D8 these walls
formed five such cells. They were separated from a similar group of cells in the southern
half of the trench and extending into the neighboring trench (D10) by an earthen surface
(assigned locus 93) measuring approximately 2 meters north-south by 3.5 meters east-
west. Surface L93 was characterized by several concentrations of ash, flat lying late
Ubaid ceramic sherds and, in the southwest corner, a concentration of yellowish clay. A
large concentration of stones in a fine silt matrix was discovered lying directly on the
eastern portion of surface L93. A variety of ceramics, lithics and a few animal bones were
discovered within this concentration of stones. A carbon sample taken from this surface
yielded a 2-sigma calibrated date of 4700-4460 BCE (Table 1).

4.15  Kaneš
227
4.15  Kaneš
The identification of Kaneš with Kültepe near Kayseri has been widely accepted for almost
a century,857 and it serves as one of the few fixed points in the historical geography of Ana-
tolia in the Colony Period. Several studies of the administration, cultural traditions, and
economic and social organisation of the city during the Old Assyrian period have been pub-
lished over the years,858 and the present section offers only a few remarks about the history
857.	 In Landsberger 1925: 5 one still reads: …‘so muß die aus der eventuellen Gleichung Kaniš = Kül Tepe zu entne-
hmende Lagebestimming noch mit abwartender Skepsis betrachtet werden’. Two years after conducting his exca-
vations at Kültepe Hrozný 1927: 11-12 identifies the site with ancient Kaneš.
858.	 For a comprehensive overview of the field cf. T. Özgüç 2003; Veenhof 2008a. Note the following additions: the
text kt m/k 3 may imply that a man named Papala ruled Kaneš during the period contemporary with the II level of
occupation in the lower city at Kültepe. The text refers to a gift of one very fine thin textile and a seal made of
lapis lazuli of the finest quality, and it mentions an oath, a sikkātum and the brother and sister of Papala. He could
also simply be a local dignitary. ICK 1, 178 shows that a certain Labarša seized kingship in Kaneš during the II
period. KTK 10 implies that a man named Kuku may have ruled Kaneš during the II-period as well. The texts
ATHE 62; ATHE 66; CCT 6, 34a and perhaps also Kay 2300; ICK 1, 13a; kt m/k 24 and kt m/k 35 show that Kaneš
had a ruling queen during the II-period. The texts kt 91/k 282; kt 93/k 145; kt 94/k 1692; kt n/k 504 and KTK 106
all refer to the ruling couple. The text kt 94/k 831 quotes an unnamed ruler of Kaneš. OIP 27,13 (cf. Dercksen
2001: 44) from Alişar would prove that Kaneš had a ruling queen at some point during the Ib period, possibly
during a war with Šalahšuwa (cf. 4.11), but this is based upon a restoration and is rejected in fn. XX(16) below. A
number of texts that record loans made to local Anatolians are dated according to particular events. In addition to
ICK 1, 178 mentioned above, which is dated to the time: ‘When Labarša seized kingship’ (l. 2-4: i-nu-me Lá-ba-
ar-ša ru-ba-ú-tám iṣ-bu-tù-ni), similar formulae are found in kt j/k 9: ‘When the king went into the qaššum of the
temple of Anna’ (l. 11-12: i-nu-me ru-ba-um a-na É A-na a-na qá-ší-im e-ru-bu, cf. also RIMA.0.33.4: 16); kt n/k
1716b: ‘When the king went out of the temple of Nippas’ (l. 9-11: i-nu-me ru-ba-um i-na É Ni-pá-as ú-ṣí-a-ni)
(dated REL 97 = 1873); kt d/k 46: ‘When the king went out of the of the temple of Nippas’ (5-7: i-nu-me ru-ba-um
i-na É Ni-pá-as ú-ṣí-a-ni); kt 88/k 90: ‘When the king came in to … the harvest’ (l. 10-12: i-nu-me ru-ba-um a-na
hu-zi-nim e-ṣa-dim e-ru-bu, cf. Dercksen 2005b n. 38). Two additional examples refer to political events (prob-
ably in Kaneš) to date a transaction: KTS 1, 50c: ‘When the queen of Wahšušana entered’ (l. 1-3: i-nu-me ru-ba-
tum Wa-ah-šu-ša-na-i-tù te9-ru-ba-ni); kt 94/k 1128: ‘When the king went to Hanaknak’ (1-3: i-nu-me ru-ba-um
a-na Ha-na-ak-na-ak i-li-ku). Following the suggestion of Forlanini 1995 based on the text kt n/k 32 (cf. also
Veenhof 2008a; Barjamovic, Hertel & Larsen in press and contra Kryszat 2008b), Hurmeli ruled Kaneš during
the Ib period and before Inar. Since the text OIP 27, 53 from Alişar mentions Hurmeli’s rabi simmilitim and was
found in level 10 T at Alişar (cf. Kryszat 2008b: 200-201), this would either date the stratum at Alişar to the very
early Ib period (which Dercksen 2001 showed is not the case) or else the text was an antique by the time it was
deposited. The witness ‘Kikaršan priest of Higiša’ occurs both in OIP 27, 53 and in kt n/k 32, showing that the
two texts must date to the same generation. The reference to both Kikaršan and Harpatiwa implies that Alişar was
under the rule of Kaneš early in the Ib period. This does not fit with the date for KTK 10 proposed by Larsen 1972
in which Amkuwa acts as an independent player in a regional alliance, and the text should be dated to the II-peri-
od (cf. 5.10). Much has recently been made out of the fact that a ruler of Harsamna bore the name Hurmeli (For-

THE ASSYRIAN COUNTRYSIDE
23 5
234
J. N. POSTGATE
facts, and since the author has not discussed the rural conditions in the
early part of the Neo-Assyrian empire, it may be of interest here to
survey the historical evidence for settlement on the North Mesopo-
tamian plains, albeit only briefly.
Let us first confess that we know remarkably little of conditions
in North Mesopotamia during the Middle Assyrian period. Diakonoff's
conclusions apply to the immediate surroundings of the city of Assur,
where both political, historical, and agricultural conditions are quite
distinct from those further north. We should not therefore assume
without proof that settlement in North Mesopotamia conformed to
patterns round Assur. However, the situation in Middle Assyrian times
in fact has very little to do with Neo-Assyrian conditions, for we know
that a radical change in the settlement of North Mesopotamia took
place in the interval. The `dark ages' around moo B.C. saw the greater
part of Assyrian territory overrun by Aramaean tribes, and a simul-
taneous intrusion of non-Semitic groups from the mountains (e.g. at
Tell Billa, as can be deduced from the personal names of its local
population in the 9th century). Although the details of this process are
understandably mostly lost to us, we can see the results of the incursions
from the situation confronting the Assyrian kings when they sought to
reassert their authority over the lands of North Mesopotamia.
The main political centres opposing this revival of the Assyrian
empire were Nasibina and Guzâna (encountered by Adad-rürâri II),
Amedi (Bit-zamâni, Assur-nasir-ap1i II), and Til-Barsip (Bit-adini
Shalmaneser III). A characteristic of these Aramaean states is their
designation as bit PN : the type of geographical name is well known, and
is found throughout the Near East wherever there were new states
founded by the Aramaean dynasties. The word bitu (or rather its Aramaic
equivalent) cannot be translated, but in the Assyrian annals, and no
doubt in Aramaic usage as well, it has principally a political meaning,
but also carries a geographical connotation. The bitu is more than a mere
tribe, which might move at any time from one district to another, but
its association with a personal name brings home the fact that the
political and geographical entity is founded on a tribal system. A more
than superficial comparison can be found in the situation in Northern
Mesopotamia shortly after the Arab conquests, when the province of
the Jazira was divided into the `abodes' (diydr) of three major tribes: the
Diyâr Bakr (Amida and district), Diyâr Mudar (the Middle Euphrates
with Harran and Raqqa), and Diyâr Rabi`a (the eastern Jazira with
Mosul and Mardin); cf. The Encyclopaedia of Islam Vol. II 343 ff.
Although the new Aramaean inhabitants were masters of most of the
lands of North Mesopotamia, this does not imply that they were settled
there. Certainly each state centred on a chief city—Nasibina, Guzâna,
etc.—but there is little evidence that outside the fortified capitals there
was any extensive new Aramaean settlement. Signs of a recent nomadic
past are still apparent : Nnr-Adad of Nasibina had a `golden tent,
symbol of his royalty' which was captured by Adad-nirâri II, and when
the same king took Guzâna, which seems to have been the capital of
Bit-Baljidni, he says that it was `held' (ukallûpi) by Abi-salâmu, mdr
Bahidni, implying, I believe, that it was not his permanent residence, and
that for at least part of the year Abi-salâmu pursued a nomadic life.
The capital cities we have mentioned seem all to have been newly
founded, or at least renamed. In this they contrast with other towns,
which survived from Middle Assyrian times through the period of
unrest and into the Neo-Assyrian period. The distribution of such towns
is significant: they lie almost exclusively on the major rivers. Down the
Euphrates we have towns like Carchemish, Hindâna, `Ana, and Hit, and
nearer to Assyria on the Habur a cluster of towns survives the dark age-
Dnr-Iaggitlim (= Dnr-aduklimmu), Sadikanni, Tâbta, Qatni, Kahat,
Urakka, and possibly also Nahur and Asnakku (cf. R: Borger ZA 62
(1972) 136). For the Balih we have less evidence, but of course Harran
remained important. The survival of these cities—mostly from the early
2nd millennium, indeed-shows that settlements along the rivers were
more tenacious in face of the chaotic conditions than those in the
intervening countryside. Apart from the additional natural protection
afforded by a river-side site, the greater fertility of the river valleys
and the constant presence of water allowed the growth of larger towns,
which could support a population capable of self-defence and able to

century date for Alahan. Very briefly, it is based on the two dated tombs in the
cemetery and their association with the West Basilica, of which the architecture,
ornament and site (at the entrance to the whole complex and in consequent
proximity to the primitive cave-church and a warren of rock-cut cells) suggest that
the monastery was a going concern in the reign of Leo I. 7 Tarasis the Elder,
6 KTric•Y
Ta -raavryrni•pia,
died on 13th February 462, and the rest of the work
must have been virtually complete within a generation. The sanctity of Tarasis
made Alahan a place of pilgrimage, for how otherwise can the hospice, the
baptistery, the shrine opposite Tarasis' tomb, and the magnificent new church
(Kodja Kalessi!) be explained? It is my belief that the Isaurian Emperor Zeno
made this grandiose scheme financially viable, and that the impetus was lost only
on the accession of the thrifty Anastasius in 491. Neither coins, pottery nor
architectural motives suggest a Justinianic date, and in any case Procopius'
statement"
povao-ripIov Ei -rT 'A-rdrva E v
'Ia••pia daVEVEocr-ro can hardly be
thought to apply to a building or buildings which do not seem to have been
restored at any time. Forsyth's suggestion of a Justinianic date for Alahan was
made before the monastery had been excavated. However, he accepted Guyer's
Zenonian dating for Meryemlik, and it would seem not unreasonable to conclude
that Dag Pazan, with which it has so much in common, was probably built at the
same time.
If Zeno really was active in ecclesiastical building in Isauria, it may not only
reflect a natural interest in the land of his fathers. An equally cogent reason may
be sought perhaps in the great theological dispute of his time. In 45 1, the decisions
of the Council of Chalcedon had rocked the Church to its foundations. Marcianus
was quite prepared to accept Pope Leo's "Tome", for he was an Orthodox and
opposed to Monophysitism; yet even so, as a sort of quid pro quo, the Patriarchate
of Constantinople was upgraded to the disadvantage and consequent displeasure
of the older and originally senior Patriarchates of Alexandria and Antioch. Egypt
and Syria therefore came out on the side of the Monophysites, and the Coptic
Church has stuck to its guns ever since. As a corollary, the ready submission of the
Egyptian Christians to the Arabs in the seventh century was in part due to the
Islamic belief in the single Person in Allah.
A divided Christendom was clearly an embarrassment to Zeno, who in 482
produced his Henotikon, a compromise document designed to please both Ortho-
dox and Monophysite; but, as is the common fate of a compromise, it was
doomed to be condemned by both parties almost from the start. Doomed it was.
but the new building programme in Isauria must have done something to cheer
both parties to the dispute in one province at least, and I believe that we may have
further evidence of Zeno's activity in the dissident Monophysite world in some
mosaics which seem to belong both psychologically and even stylistically to the
period of the Henotikon.
This ill-starred document was intended to herald a
period of reconciliation, not of absolute agreement between the factions, the sort
of reconciliation foreshadowed in Isaiah's often misquoted prophecy: "The wolf

AN APPRECIATION OF FRANK CALVERT (1828-1908)
continued his research. Although he kept himself available to Schliemann and
occasionally excavated a few sites, some of them at Schliemann's expense,
Calvert and Schliemann were increasingly going their separate ways. Having
ceded the archaeology of the Troad to Schliemann, Calvert turned his attention
to other scientific matters. He continued to collaborate with Virchow and
Ascherson, and met and corresponded with eminent men of his day. He did not
tell Schliemann much about these activities.
Calvert's ongoing independent work resulted in a number of studies on
diverse subjects, although his busy life and the lack of facilities at the
Dardanelles occasionally kept him from writing as detailed a study as he would
have liked. In addition to his archaeological papers, Calvert wrote about the
geology, geography, agronomy, botany, paleontology, and entomology of the
Troad. Several of his studies appeared as letters or short articles in a variety of
publications, many of which were too specialized to be read by the general pub-
lic. Others were never published. As a result, Calvert's contributions to these
fields remain largely unknown.
For as long as he owned the land at Hissarlik, Calvert continued to be
concerned about its preservation. After Schliemann, whom he outlived by 18
years, he stood by to assist Schliemann's successor, Wilhelm D6rpfeld. When
too many tourists walking over the site threatened to damage it, he openly dis-
couraged them from going there. When age caught up with him, he donated
the site to the Turkish Government, and seemed pleased to be officially reward-
ed for his gift.
An assessment of Calvert
For all his activities, including his studies, Calvert's schooling and the
knowledge and experience he had attained through his reading and field work
had almost always been sufficient. When he needed additional information, he
asked acquaintances abroad to send it to him, once or twice delaying publica-
tion of a paper until he received the necessary material. When he occasionally
lacked some technical knowledge, he openly admitted it. At worst, he consid-
ered these deficiencies something of a nuisance, but he had never been embar-
rassed by them.
His years of field work and diligent study had given him an expertise that
was unrivalled for this time, and he had successfully applied his knowledge to
the further understanding of the Troad. Omitting Schliemann, and with the
possible exception of Newton, Calvert had gained the respect of every formally
trained, and often celebrated scholar with whom he came into contact in per-
son, by letter, or through his published writings. In much the same manner as
he, at least two of his brothers had attained comparable degrees of mastery and
renown in their own respective fields.
Under ordinary circumstances, therefore, Calvert would never have become
self-conscious about having left school at 16, the same age as his brothers. The
fact that he did so must be attributed to Schliemann's impact on him, if for no
other reason than that there is no evidence of such feelings in Calvert before
Schliemann's arrival in the Troad.
Perhaps Calvert had begun to measure himself against Schliemann and his
doctorate, and to contrast himself to scholars like Virchow with whom he was
now increasingly in contact. Perhaps Calvert perceived himself insufficiently
qualified compared to the cosmopolitan backgrounds of these scholars, and was
too intimidated by their reputations to compare the value of a diploma against
that of his own unrivalled expertise, which these same scholars openly admired.

5.17  The Geography North and West of Kaneš
377
identifies the grid of routes and road-knots that may be compared with the fixed points, the
topographical setting and the available archaeological evidence (discussed in 2.8). Unlike the
area east of Kaneš, where the regional topography played an important role in the reconstruc-
tion of the historical geography and the identification of the main trade routes, the Central
Anatolian Plateau is a more open landscape and the traditional routes are less constrained by
their surroundings. Fortunately, the number of fixed points in the region is much higher than
in the Taurus and the archaeology is known in greater detail. This compensates for the loss of
topographical information. In addition, the landscape continues to dictate the flow of traffic
to some extent (3.4). Wide rivers and dry wastes have always hindered movement and given
preference to certain routes.

A diagram showing all the geographical relations west of Kaneš attested in the Old Assyr-
ian texts have been combined into diagram XX and joined to the network of routes east of the
city (4.14). The site of Kültepe is not the only fixed point in this system, and the position of
the route running east of Kaneš, the harrān sūqinnim (4.9), allows the networks east and north
of Kaneš to be located according to one another. One can orient the grid east of Kaneš in rela-
tion to the fixed position at Karahna (5.5) on the route from Hurama and Šamuha in the east
(4.6) to Kuburnat and Durhumit in the north (5.1).

The location of Karahna at Sulusaray 30-35 km west of the modern road leading from Si-
vas to Tokat suggests that the Assyrian merchants may have used a pass over the Ak Dağlar
west of Yıldızeli. If the identification of Šamuha with Kayalıpınar is correct, a pass that
emerges at the village of Üyük north of the mountains would lead from the Kızılırmak in an
almost straight line to Sulusaray (map XX(43)).

epilogue of LH (col. V:16, where it is said to be Marduk's instruction to the king; cf. also
XLVII:62 and 73), it is difficult to prove that the writer of the letter actually quotes these
words. The appointment of Babylon's king by Marduk and his duty of maintaning justice and
restoring equity are essential and well known elements of royal ideology, not restricted to the
text of law collections.
These examples help us to appreciate the difference between laws and decrees. They show
the concrete impact of royal decrees in social and economic life and explain why plaintiffs,
even when appealing to the king himself and perhaps familiar with the wording of his laws,
did not or could not refer to particular stipulations in the laws. They based there appeals for
legal help on specific royal decrees and on general statements of the king's righteousness and
his duty to redress injustice, which were the generally known ideological basis of both
decrees and laws.

Description
Broken left. Complete above, below and right, but the surface is deeply broken off
below and right. H. 48 cm. (surface 42 cm.), W. 49 cm. (surface 30 cm.), D. at least 50 cm.
Letters average about 1 9 cm. Upper margin 3 5 cm.
Position
The height of the fragment and of the upper margin are indicative of the Physics, and
this attribution is confirmed by the subject-matter.
Diogenes is evidently refuting the view that the events of the world are governed by
divine agency, and that the gods are beings to be feared and appeased. The fragment may
have stood close to NF 39 and 40, in which Diogenes argues, in opposition to the Stoics,
that the gods did not create the world for their own sakes or for the sake of mankind.

19) Raining fish – In his article ‘The sign of the Flood' (Iraq 49 (1987): 63-69), A. R. Millard has discussed
the well-known Gilgamesh passage that refers to the raining down of fish, and suggested that this should be
interpreted as an ominous sign of what was to come, concluding that it reflected an omen tradition based on
the observation of freak reality. In support of this he commented that ‘meteorological records from many parts
of the world include occasions when fish fell during storms,' but only mentions as a specific case an
occurrence in South Wales in 1859. Those interested in such phenomena will read with profit the detailed
study by Sunder Lal Hora entitled ‘Rains of fish in India,' Journal and Proceedings of the Asiatic Society of
Bengal. New Series 29 (1933): 95-110, and the follow-up article by S. N. Sen entitled ‘The Meteorological
Aspects of the recent ‘Rains of Fishes' in the Muzaffarpur District,' ibid. 111-116. These do much to lend
support to Millard's interpretation.
I. L. FINKEL (07-03-2002)
20) – Nouvelles données pour la chronologie du IIe millénaire – La longue séquence dendrochronologique
de plus de mille ans établie sur des échantillons de bois provenant de nombreux sites anatoliens vient d'être
affinée grâce à des analyses de radiocarbone de haute précision. Celles-ci ont permis aux spécialistes de reculer
cette séquence dendrochronologique de 22 ans (+4 ou -7 ans) sur une échelle absolue (P.I. Kuniholm et alii,
Nature 381 [1996], p. 780–783 et S. W. Manning et alii, Science 294 [21 décembre 2001], p. 2532–2535). Dès
lors, les constructions des palais d'Acemhöyük (Sarÿkaya) et de Kültepe (dit «palais de Warßamma∞)
dateraient respectivement de 1778/1767 et 1836/1825 av. J.-C., le second ayant subi des réparations 61 ans
plus tard, en 1775/1764 av. J.-C.
Les premiers résultats de dendrochronologie ont été confrontés à la mention d'une éclipse de soleil
dans les Chroniques Éponymales de Mari (CEM), observée l'année suivant la naissance de Samsî-Addu,
pendant l'éponymie de Puzur-Ißtar (C. Michel et P. Rocher, JEOL 35-36 [1997-2000], p. 111-126). Après
examen des 34 éclipses visibles sur l'Irak entre 1850 et 1740 av. J.-C., deux d'entre elles seulement pouvaient
correspondre à celle mentionnée dans CEM: les éclipses survenues en 1795 et 1744 av. J.-C. La première,
semblant mieux convenir, permettait de situer la naissance de Samsî-Addu en 1796, sa conquête d'Aßßur en
1758 et son décès en 1725. Ce résultat abaissait la chronologie moyenne de 51 ans. Les nouvelles données
dendrochronologiques impliquent naturellement une révision de ces conclusions. De toutes les éclipses
analysées, cinq pouvaient être prises en considération. Les trois premières, 1833, 1795, 1744 av. J.-C. étaient
visibles dans leur phase de centralité à Aßßur et à Bagdad, la quatrième, 1765 av. J.-C. n'a pu être observée qu'à
Aßßur, et la cinquième, 1741 av. J.-C., était visible sur l'Irak en sa phase partielle.
Les éclipses de 1744 et 1741 av. J.-C., bien que possibles, paraissaient déjà trop basses lors de leur
confrontation avec les premières données dendrochonologiques. Dès lors que ces dernières doivent être
reculées dans le temps de 22 ans, la probabilité que l'une de ces deux éclipses corresponde à celle mentionnée
dans CEM diminue encore. L'éclipse observée à Aßßur en 1765 av. J.-C. place la construction du palais
d'Acemhöyük et les réparations de celui de Kültepe environ une vingtaine d'années avant la fin du kårum II de
Kaniß. Cet écart correspond exactement à celui fourni par la confrontation de l'éclipse de 1744 av. J.-C. avec les
premières données dendrochronologiques (C. Michel et P. Rocher, op. cit., p. 121): il nous avait alors paru peu
vraisemblable.
