page_text:
1.
Sumerian Early Dynastic (G. Magid): nos. 1-13;
2.
Old Akkadian (B. Studevent-Hickman, C. Morgan): nos. 14-22;
3.
Gudea/Ur III (R. Averbeck, B. Studevent-Hickman, P.
Michalowski): nos. 23-41;
4-5. Old Babylonian (F. van Koppen): nos. 42-55, 56-76;
6.
Kassite/Middle Assyrian/Middle Babylonian/Alalah/Emar (F.
van Koppen, K. Greenwood, C. Morgan, B.A. Strawn, J. Coo-
ley, B.T. Arnold, E. von Dassow, Y. Cohen): nos. 77-90;
7.
Amarna letters (E. von Dassow, K. Greenwood): nos. 91-103;
8.
Hittite Empire (G. Beckman, P. Goedegebuure, J. Hazenbos,
Y. Cohen): nos. 104-111;
9.
Hittite Empire (K.R. Mineck, T. van den Hout, H.A. Hoffner
Jr.): nos. 112-117;
10. Neo-Assyrian and Syro-Palestinian (S.C. Melville, B.A.
Strawn, B.B. Schmidt, S. Noegel): nos. 118-126;
11. Neo-Assyrian and Syro-Palestinian (B.A. Strawn, S.C.
Melville, K. Greenwood, S. Noegel): nos. 127-142;
12. Neo-Babylonian (B. Studevent-Hickman, S.C. Melville, S.
Noegel): nos. 143-149;
13. Achaemenid (B.T. Arnold, P. Michalowski): nos. 150-157.
The book was written ‘with the intention that it will be
considered a readable and affordable anthology of ancient
Near Eastern historical texts in translation’. It is meant to be
used as a primary sourcebook in undergraduate courses in
ancient Near Eastern history. But most of the pages are
devoted to Mesopotamia and Anatolia, with only a secondary
interest for Syria-Palestine and even less for Egypt. Elam and
Urartu appear only as enemies of the Neo-Assyrian Empire.
Because the definition of the ancient Near East thus implied
and the range of historical periods covered is limited, the
question is raised of who the intended users are; for many
students it may become an introduction to the history of
Mesopotamia and the neighbouring countries.
The texts selected to illuminate the major periods of
Mesopotamian and Syro-Palestinian history include Gudea
Statue B (no.24), the Sumerian King List (no.40), the
Dadusha Stele (no.51), the Shitti-Marduk Stele (no.86), the
Anitta Text (no.104), Hattusili I’s Testament (no.106), the
Sfire Inscription (no.123), the Mesha Stele (no.126), Sargon’s
Eighth Campaign (no.128), Sennacherib’s Annals (no.130),
Babylonian Chronicles (nos.150-156) and the Cyrus Cylin-
der (no.157). Two periods that ought to have been included
are lacking, although they are included in a recently published
comparable volume (see below). First, the Early Dynastic
period must really incorporate some texts from Ebla; some
of the epigraphic finds excavated there, like the letter of
Enna-Dagan or the one mentioning Hamazi, might have
enriched the book. Secondly, the Old Assyrian period is
almost completely left out of consideration. The trade
between Assur and Anatolia is mentioned twice and two per-
tinent publications are listed in bibliographical sections
(pp.41, 219), but this serves merely to sketch the context of
the legendary composition Sargon, King of Battle (no. 20)
and that of the Anitta Text (no. 104). Judging from this col-
lection, authentic sources from Anatolia seem to start only
with the Hittites, despite the availability of the letter of
Anuhirwe to King Warshama of Kanesh and the occurrence
of Anitta and his father Pithana as kings in Anatolian legal
documents (written in Akkadian) that have been discovered
in Kanesh and Alishar. Babylonian history does not stop with
Cyrus; from the Seleucid period there are important inscrip-
tions and astronomical diaries that could have been included
here to illustrate the continuity of Babylonian culture long
after the end of the Neo-Babylonian Empire.
Such a sourcebook should adhere to the standard conven-
tions for scholarly publications, that is, an edition with trans-
lations that are reliable and complete and indications of which
parts of the translated text are broken or restored, and refer-
ences to original text publications or standard editions. There
should also be a measure of consistency in the rendering of
names and terms. Beyond that, the book should stimulate the
reader’s critical handling of texts by providing clear intro-
ductions and commentaries with explanatory notes that
include references to up-to-date publications.
The general format of the book is attractive, with each
chapter or subdivision starting with an introduction to the
period or genre of text, followed by the texts in translation
and a bibliography. However, the accessibility of the work
might have been greater if some issues (mainly of an edito-
rial nature) had been arranged differently. Contributors were
apparently allowed the liberty to format their own chapters
as they wished. At times the endnotes (footnotes would have
been easier to consult) are extremely detailed and the bibli-
ographic information abundant. The way the original edition
of a text should be cited is exemplified in the contributions
of F. van Koppen, but there is no agreed style elsewhere in
this book. Some chapters lack any clear indication of the
siglum for the text. The unprepared reader may find some
puzzling abbreviations; for example, Ki 3.1 on p.5, which
turns out to be the number of the text in the edition of J.S.
Cooper, Sumerian and Akkadian Royal Inscriptions I. Line
numbers are not given; this may have little consequence for
beginners, but it is essential for checking a translation.
Restored or missing lines are not always properly indicated
as such. The use of more cross-references would have facil-
itated finding related information; now it can often be done
only through the imperfect Index (see below).
The endnotes are used to a greater or lesser extent to pro-
vide additional information or to justify a translation, but
there are none in Melville’s treatment of Neo-Assyrian royal
texts. In the annals of Sennacherib (no.130) the word Urbu
is translated Arabs, which demands a note to show that this
interpretation is far from certain (see Frahm, AfO Beiheft 26,
104f.).
The structure and content of the various bibliographies is
not always satisfactory and a different arrangement would
have increased their usefulness and avoided repetitions. There
was a good opportunity in this anthology to provide students
with a list of standard reference works; G. Leick, Who’s Who
in the Ancient Near East, J. Black and A. Green, Gods,
Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia and the series
RIME etc. and RGTC are referred to, but other works, such
as the Reallexikon der Assyriologie, are rarely used, and some
not at all, such as A.R. George, House Most High. The last
work could have been mentioned to explain Esarra (Assur
temple at Assur, p.379 n.1; part of the Ekur at Nippur, p.164),
and Esagila p.405 n.8 (instead of the odd reference to Gen.
11:4). The bibliography of the section with alphabetic texts
from Syria-Palestine refers to the edition in KAI for no.123
(Sfire), but not for no.125 (Zakkur = KAI 202) or for no.126
(Mesha = KAI 181). J.-J. Glassner’s edition of Mesopotamian
chronicles is used in the original French edition (p.422), as
well as in the English translation (p.87).
A flaw in this book is the lack of recognizable logic in the
sequence in which concepts are explained. For example, the
Babylonian (and Assyrian) New Year festival and the special
building it was celebrated in, the akitu and the bit akiti in text
321
